results:
Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code IDE integration with file and tab operations in VS Code and Cursor:

## File and Tab Opening in IDE Integration

Dragging files in normally opens them in a new tab like in Cursor or VS Code. However, holding Shift while dragging will reference them properly in Claude.

## Claude Code IDE Integration Status (2026)

Claude Code now runs in VS Code, JetBrains, a desktop app, and a browser-based IDE at claude.ai/code. The Claude Code extension works with VS Code, Cursor, and probably Windsurf too.

## Key Features with File Context

The VS Code extension provides several ways to work with files:

- You can @-mention files and folders by typing @ followed by a filename, with Claude supporting fuzzy matching like @auth.ts to match auth.ts or AuthService.ts.

- Current selection/tab is automatically shared with Claude Code.

- You can open the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P) and select "Claude Code: Open in New Tab" to run parallel conversations for different tasks.

## Installation

In VS Code, press Cmd+Shift+X (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+X (Windows/Linux) to open the Extensions view, search for "Claude Code", and click Install. The extension also works with Cursor, Windsurf, and VSCodium.

input.prompt: What can Claude Code do inside VS Code? Can it open files, open tabs, control the editor? Can it use the VS Code API to open files in specific editor groups or splits? List every capability related to file opening, tab management, and editor control. Also look for any limitations - things Claude Code explicitly CANNOT do in the IDE.

content.content: > ## Documentation Index > Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt > Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further. # Use Claude Code in VS Code > Install and configure the Claude Code extension for VS Code. Get AI coding assistance with inline diffs, @-mentions, plan review, and keyboard shortcuts. The VS Code extension provides a native graphical interface for Claude Code, integrated directly into your IDE. This is the recommended way to use Claude Code in VS Code. With the extension, you can review and edit Claude's plans before accepting them, auto-accept edits as they're made, @-mention files with specific line ranges from your selection, access conversation history, and open multiple conversations in separate tabs or windows. ## Prerequisites Before installing, make sure you have: * VS Code 1.98.0 or higher * An Anthropic account (you'll sign in when you first open the extension). If you're using a third-party provider like Amazon Bedrock or Google Vertex AI, see [Use third-party providers](#use-third-party-providers) instead. The extension includes the CLI (command-line interface), which you can access from VS Code's integrated terminal for advanced features. See [VS Code extension vs. Claude Code CLI](#vs-code-extension-vs-claude-code-cli) for details. ## Install the extension Click the link for your IDE to install directly: * [Install for VS Code](vscode:extension/anthropic.claude-code) * [Install for Cursor](cursor:extension/anthropic.claude-code) Or in VS Code, press `Cmd+Shift+X` (Mac) or `Ctrl+Shift+X` (Windows/Linux) to open the Extensions view, search for "Claude Code", and click **Install**. If the extension doesn't appear after installation, restart VS Code or run "Developer: Reload Window" from the Command Palette. ## Get started Once installed, you can start using Claude Code through the VS Code interface: Throughout VS Code, the Spark icon indicates Claude Code: The quickest way to open Claude is to click the Spark icon in the **Editor Toolbar** (top-right corner of the editor). The icon only appears when you have a file open. Other ways to open Claude Code: * **Activity Bar**: click the Spark icon in the left sidebar to open the sessions list. Click any session to open it as a full editor tab, or start a new one. This icon is always visible in the Activity Bar. * **Command Palette**: `Cmd+Shift+P` (Mac) or `Ctrl+Shift+P` (Windows/Linux), type "Claude Code", and select an option like "Open in New Tab" * **Status Bar**: click **✱ Claude Code** in the bottom-right corner of the window. This works even when no file is open. When you first open the panel, a **Learn Claude Code** checklist appears. Work through each item by clicking **Show me**, or dismiss it with the X. To reopen it later, uncheck **Hide Onboarding** in VS Code settings under Extensions → Claude Code. You can drag the Claude panel to reposition it anywhere in VS Code. See [Customize your workflow](#customize-your-workflow) for details. Ask Claude to help with your code or files, whether that's explaining how something works, debugging an issue, or making changes. Claude automatically sees your selected text. Press `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) to also insert an @-mention reference (like `@file.ts#5-10`) into your prompt. Here's an example of asking about a particular line in a file: When Claude wants to edit a file, it shows a side-by-side comparison of the original and proposed changes, then asks for permission. You can accept, reject, or tell Claude what to do instead. For more ideas on what you can do with Claude Code, see [Common workflows](/en/common-workflows). Run "Claude Code: Open Walkthrough" from the Command Palette for a guided tour of the basics. ## Use the prompt box The prompt box supports several features: * **Permission modes**: click the mode indicator at the bottom of the prompt box to switch modes. In normal mode, Claude asks permission before each action. In Plan mode, Claude describes what it will do and waits for approval before making changes. VS Code automatically opens the plan as a full markdown document where you can add inline comments to give feedback before Claude begins. In auto-accept mode, Claude makes edits without asking. Set the default in VS Code settings under `claudeCode.initialPermissionMode`. * **Command menu**: click `/` or type `/` to open the command menu. Options include attaching files, switching models, toggling extended thinking, and viewing plan usage (`/usage`). The Customize section provides access to MCP servers, hooks, memory, permissions, and plugins. Items with a terminal icon open in the integrated terminal. * **Context indicator**: the prompt box shows how much of Claude's context window you're using. Claude automatically compacts when needed, or you can run `/compact` manually. * **Extended thinking**: lets Claude spend more time reasoning through complex problems. Toggle it on via the command menu (`/`). See [Extended thinking](/en/common-workflows#use-extended-thinking-thinking-mode) for details. * **Multi-line input**: press `Shift+Enter` to add a new line without sending. This also works in the "Other" free-text input of question dialogs. ### Reference files and folders Use @-mentions to give Claude context about specific files or folders. When you type `@` followed by a file or folder name, Claude reads that content and can answer questions about it or make changes to it. Claude Code supports fuzzy matching, so you can type partial names to find what you need: ```text theme={null} > Explain the logic in @auth (fuzzy matches auth.js, AuthService.ts, etc.) > What's in @src/components/ (include a trailing slash for folders) ``` For large PDFs, you can ask Claude to read specific pages instead of the whole file: a single page, a range like pages 1-10, or an open-ended range like page 3 onward. When you select text in the editor, Claude can see your highlighted code automatically. The prompt box footer shows how many lines are selected. Press `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) to insert an @-mention with the file path and line numbers (e.g., `@app.ts#5-10`). Click the selection indicator to toggle whether Claude can see your highlighted text - the eye-slash icon means the selection is hidden from Claude. You can also hold `Shift` while dragging files into the prompt box to add them as attachments. Click the X on any attachment to remove it from context. ### Resume past conversations Click the dropdown at the top of the Claude Code panel to access your conversation history. You can search by keyword or browse by time (Today, Yesterday, Last 7 days, etc.). Click any conversation to resume it with the full message history. Hover over a session to reveal rename and remove actions: rename to give it a descriptive title, or remove to delete it from the list. For more on resuming sessions, see [Common workflows](/en/common-workflows#resume-previous-conversations). ### Resume remote sessions from Claude.ai If you use [Claude Code on the web](/en/claude-code-on-the-web), you can resume those remote sessions directly in VS Code. This requires signing in with **Claude.ai Subscription**, not Anthropic Console. Click the **Past Conversations** dropdown at the top of the Claude Code panel. The dialog shows two tabs: Local and Remote. Click **Remote** to see sessions from claude.ai. Browse or search your remote sessions. Click any session to download it and continue the conversation locally. Only web sessions started with a GitHub repository appear in the Remote tab. Resuming loads the conversation history locally; changes are not synced back to claude.ai. ## Customize your workflow Once you're up and running, you can reposition the Claude panel, run multiple sessions, or switch to terminal mode. ### Choose where Claude lives You can drag the Claude panel to reposition it anywhere in VS Code. Grab the panel's tab or title bar and drag it to: * **Secondary sidebar**: the right side of the window. Keeps Claude visible while you code. * **Primary sidebar**: the left sidebar with icons for Explorer, Search, etc. * **Editor area**: opens Claude as a tab alongside your files. Useful for side tasks. Use the sidebar for your main Claude session and open additional tabs for side tasks. Claude remembers your preferred location. The Activity Bar sessions list icon is separate from the Claude panel: the sessions list is always visible in the Activity Bar, while the Claude panel icon only appears there when the panel is docked to the left sidebar. ### Run multiple conversations Use **Open in New Tab** or **Open in New Window** from the Command Palette to start additional conversations. Each conversation maintains its own history and context, allowing you to work on different tasks in parallel. When using tabs, a small colored dot on the spark icon indicates status: blue means a permission request is pending, orange means Claude finished while the tab was hidden. ### Switch to terminal mode By default, the extension opens a graphical chat panel. If you prefer the CLI-style interface, open the [Use Terminal setting](vscode://settings/claudeCode.useTerminal) and check the box. You can also open VS Code settings (`Cmd+,` on Mac or `Ctrl+,` on Windows/Linux), go to Extensions → Claude Code, and check **Use Terminal**. ## Manage plugins The VS Code extension includes a graphical interface for installing and managing [plugins](/en/plugins). Type `/plugins` in the prompt box to open the **Manage plugins** interface. ### Install plugins The plugin dialog shows two tabs: **Plugins** and **Marketplaces**. In the Plugins tab: * **Installed plugins** appear at the top with toggle switches to enable or disable them * **Available plugins** from your configured marketplaces appear below * Search to filter plugins by name or description * Click **Install** on any available plugin When you install a plugin, choose the installation scope: * **Install for you**: available in all your projects (user scope) * **Install for this project**: shared with project collaborators (project scope) * **Install locally**: only for you, only in this repository (local scope) ### Manage marketplaces Switch to the **Marketplaces** tab to add or remove plugin sources: * Enter a GitHub repo, URL, or local path to add a new marketplace * Click the refresh icon to update a marketplace's plugin list * Click the trash icon to remove a marketplace After making changes, a banner prompts you to restart Claude Code to apply the updates. Plugin management in VS Code uses the same CLI commands under the hood. Plugins and marketplaces you configure in the extension are also available in the CLI, and vice versa. For more about the plugin system, see [Plugins](/en/plugins) and [Plugin marketplaces](/en/plugin-marketplaces). ## Automate browser tasks with Chrome Connect Claude to your Chrome browser to test web apps, debug with console logs, and automate browser workflows without leaving VS Code. This requires the [Claude in Chrome extension](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/claude/fcoeoabgfenejglbffodgkkbkcdhcgfn) version 1.0.36 or higher. Type `@browser` in the prompt box followed by what you want Claude to do: ```text theme={null} @browser go to localhost:3000 and check the console for errors ``` You can also open the attachment menu to select specific browser tools like opening a new tab or reading page content. Claude opens new tabs for browser tasks and shares your browser's login state, so it can access any site you're already signed into. For setup instructions, the full list of capabilities, and troubleshooting, see [Use Claude Code with Chrome](/en/chrome). ## VS Code commands and shortcuts Open the Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P` on Mac or `Ctrl+Shift+P` on Windows/Linux) and type "Claude Code" to see all available VS Code commands for the Claude Code extension. Some shortcuts depend on which panel is "focused" (receiving keyboard input). When your cursor is in a code file, the editor is focused. When your cursor is in Claude's prompt box, Claude is focused. Use `Cmd+Esc` / `Ctrl+Esc` to toggle between them. These are VS Code commands for controlling the extension. Not all built-in Claude Code commands are available in the extension. See [VS Code extension vs. Claude Code CLI](#vs-code-extension-vs-claude-code-cli) for details. | Command | Shortcut | Description | | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | Focus Input | `Cmd+Esc` (Mac) / `Ctrl+Esc` (Windows/Linux) | Toggle focus between editor and Claude | | Open in Side Bar | - | Open Claude in the left sidebar | | Open in Terminal | - | Open Claude in terminal mode | | Open in New Tab | `Cmd+Shift+Esc` (Mac) / `Ctrl+Shift+Esc` (Windows/Linux) | Open a new conversation as an editor tab | | Open in New Window | - | Open a new conversation in a separate window | | New Conversation | `Cmd+N` (Mac) / `Ctrl+N` (Windows/Linux) | Start a new conversation (requires Claude to be focused) | | Insert @-Mention Reference | `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) | Insert a reference to the current file and selection (requires editor to be focused) | | Show Logs | - | View extension debug logs | | Logout | - | Sign out of your Anthropic account | ## Configure settings The extension has two types of settings: * **Extension settings** in VS Code: control the extension's behavior within VS Code. Open with `Cmd+,` (Mac) or `Ctrl+,` (Windows/Linux), then go to Extensions → Claude Code. You can also type `/` and select **General Config** to open settings. * **Claude Code settings** in `~/.claude/settings.json`: shared between the extension and CLI. Use for allowed commands, environment variables, hooks, and MCP servers. See [Settings](/en/settings) for details. Add `"$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json"` to your `settings.json` to get autocomplete and inline validation for all available settings directly in VS Code. ### Extension settings | Setting | Default | Description | | --------------------------------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `selectedModel` | `default` | Model for new conversations. Change per-session with `/model`. | | `useTerminal` | `false` | Launch Claude in terminal mode instead of graphical panel | | `initialPermissionMode` | `default` | Controls approval prompts: `default` (ask each time), `plan`, `acceptEdits`, or `bypassPermissions` | | `preferredLocation` | `panel` | Where Claude opens: `sidebar` (right) or `panel` (new tab) | | `autosave` | `true` | Auto-save files before Claude reads or writes them | | `useCtrlEnterToSend` | `false` | Use Ctrl/Cmd+Enter instead of Enter to send prompts | | `enableNewConversationShortcut` | `true` | Enable Cmd/Ctrl+N to start a new conversation | | `hideOnboarding` | `false` | Hide the onboarding checklist (graduation cap icon) | | `respectGitIgnore` | `true` | Exclude .gitignore patterns from file searches | | `environmentVariables` | `[]` | Set environment variables for the Claude process. Use Claude Code settings instead for shared config. | | `disableLoginPrompt` | `false` | Skip authentication prompts (for third-party provider setups) | | `allowDangerouslySkipPermissions` | `false` | Bypass all permission prompts. **Use with extreme caution.** | | `claudeProcessWrapper` | - | Executable path used to launch the Claude process | ## VS Code extension vs. Claude Code CLI Claude Code is available as both a VS Code extension (graphical panel) and a CLI (command-line interface in the terminal). Some features are only available in the CLI. If you need a CLI-only feature, run `claude` in VS Code's integrated terminal. | Feature | CLI | VS Code Extension | | ------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | Commands and skills | [All](/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands) | Subset (type `/` to see available) | | MCP server config | Yes | Partial (add servers via CLI; manage existing servers with `/mcp` in the chat panel) | | Checkpoints | Yes | Yes | | `!` bash shortcut | Yes | No | | Tab completion | Yes | No | ### Rewind with checkpoints The VS Code extension supports checkpoints, which track Claude's file edits and let you rewind to a previous state. Hover over any message to reveal the rewind button, then choose from three options: * **Fork conversation from here**: start a new conversation branch from this message while keeping all code changes intact * **Rewind code to here**: revert file changes back to this point in the conversation while keeping the full conversation history * **Fork conversation and rewind code**: start a new conversation branch and revert file changes to this point For full details on how checkpoints work and their limitations, see [Checkpointing](/en/checkpointing). ### Run CLI in VS Code To use the CLI while staying in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (`` Ctrl+` `` on Windows/Linux or `` Cmd+` `` on Mac) and run `claude`. The CLI automatically integrates with your IDE for features like diff viewing and diagnostic sharing. If using an external terminal, run `/ide` inside Claude Code to connect it to VS Code. ### Switch between extension and CLI The extension and CLI share the same conversation history. To continue an extension conversation in the CLI, run `claude --resume` in the terminal. This opens an interactive picker where you can search for and select your conversation. ### Include terminal output in prompts Reference terminal output in your prompts using `@terminal:name` where `name` is the terminal's title. This lets Claude see command output, error messages, or logs without copy-pasting. ### Monitor background processes When Claude runs long-running commands, the extension shows progress in the status bar. However, visibility for background tasks is limited compared to the CLI. For better visibility, have Claude output the command so you can run it in VS Code's integrated terminal. ### Connect to external tools with MCP MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers give Claude access to external tools, databases, and APIs. To add an MCP server, open the integrated terminal (`` Ctrl+` `` or `` Cmd+` ``) and run: ```bash theme={null} claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ ``` Once configured, ask Claude to use the tools (e.g., "Review PR #456"). To manage MCP servers without leaving VS Code, type `/mcp` in the chat panel. The MCP management dialog lets you enable or disable servers, reconnect to a server, and manage OAuth authentication. See the [MCP documentation](/en/mcp) for available servers. ## Work with git Claude Code integrates with git to help with version control workflows directly in VS Code. Ask Claude to commit changes, create pull requests, or work across branches. ### Create commits and pull requests Claude can stage changes, write commit messages, and create pull requests based on your work: ```text theme={null} > commit my changes with a descriptive message > create a pr for this feature > summarize the changes I've made to the auth module ``` When creating pull requests, Claude generates descriptions based on the actual code changes and can add context about testing or implementation decisions. ### Use git worktrees for parallel tasks Use the `--worktree` (`-w`) flag to start Claude in an isolated worktree with its own files and branch: ```bash theme={null} claude --worktree feature-auth ``` Each worktree maintains independent file state while sharing git history. This prevents Claude instances from interfering with each other when working on different tasks. For more details, see [Run parallel sessions with Git worktrees](/en/common-workflows#run-parallel-claude-code-sessions-with-git-worktrees). ## Use third-party providers By default, Claude Code connects directly to Anthropic's API. If your organization uses Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry to access Claude, configure the extension to use your provider instead: Open the [Disable Login Prompt setting](vscode://settings/claudeCode.disableLoginPrompt) and check the box. You can also open VS Code settings (`Cmd+,` on Mac or `Ctrl+,` on Windows/Linux), search for "Claude Code login", and check **Disable Login Prompt**. Follow the setup guide for your provider: * [Claude Code on Amazon Bedrock](/en/amazon-bedrock) * [Claude Code on Google Vertex AI](/en/google-vertex-ai) * [Claude Code on Microsoft Foundry](/en/microsoft-foundry) These guides cover configuring your provider in `~/.claude/settings.json`, which ensures your settings are shared between the VS Code extension and the CLI. ## Security and privacy Your code stays private. Claude Code processes your code to provide assistance but does not use it to train models. For details on data handling and how to opt out of logging, see [Data and privacy](/en/data-usage). With auto-edit permissions enabled, Claude Code can modify VS Code configuration files (like `settings.json` or `tasks.json`) that VS Code may execute automatically. To reduce risk when working with untrusted code: * Enable [VS Code Restricted Mode](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/workspace-trust#_restricted-mode) for untrusted workspaces * Use manual approval mode instead of auto-accept for edits * Review changes carefully before accepting them ## Fix common issues ### Extension won't install * Ensure you have a compatible version of VS Code (1.98.0 or later) * Check that VS Code has permission to install extensions * Try installing directly from the [VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anthropic.claude-code) ### Spark icon not visible The Spark icon appears in the **Editor Toolbar** (top-right of editor) when you have a file open. If you don't see it: 1. **Open a file**: The icon requires a file to be open. Having just a folder open isn't enough. 2. **Check VS Code version**: Requires 1.98.0 or higher (Help → About) 3. **Restart VS Code**: Run "Developer: Reload Window" from the Command Palette 4. **Disable conflicting extensions**: Temporarily disable other AI extensions (Cline, Continue, etc.) 5. **Check workspace trust**: The extension doesn't work in Restricted Mode Alternatively, click "✱ Claude Code" in the **Status Bar** (bottom-right corner). This works even without a file open. You can also use the **Command Palette** (`Cmd+Shift+P` / `Ctrl+Shift+P`) and type "Claude Code". ### Claude Code never responds If Claude Code isn't responding to your prompts: 1. **Check your internet connection**: Ensure you have a stable internet connection 2. **Start a new conversation**: Try starting a fresh conversation to see if the issue persists 3. **Try the CLI**: Run `claude` from the terminal to see if you get more detailed error messages If problems persist, [file an issue on GitHub](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues) with details about the error. ## Uninstall the extension To uninstall the Claude Code extension: 1. Open the Extensions view (`Cmd+Shift+X` on Mac or `Ctrl+Shift+X` on Windows/Linux) 2. Search for "Claude Code" 3. Click **Uninstall** To also remove extension data and reset all settings: ```bash theme={null} rm -rf ~/.vscode/globalStorage/anthropic.claude-code ``` For additional help, see the [troubleshooting guide](/en/troubleshooting). ## Next steps Now that you have Claude Code set up in VS Code: * [Explore common workflows](/en/common-workflows) to get the most out of Claude Code * [Set up MCP servers](/en/mcp) to extend Claude's capabilities with external tools. Add servers using the CLI, then manage them with `/mcp` in the chat panel. * [Configure Claude Code settings](/en/settings) to customize allowed commands, hooks, and more. These settings are shared between the extension and CLI.

Claude automatically sees your selected text. Press `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) to also insert an @-mention reference (like `@file.ts#5-10`) into your prompt. Here's an example of asking about a particular line in a file: When Claude wants to edit a file, it shows a side-by-side comparison of the original and proposed changes, then asks for permission. You can accept, reject, or tell Claude what to do instead. For more ideas on what you can do with Claude Code, see [Common workflows](/en/common-workflows). Run "Claude Code: Open Walkthrough" from the Command Palette for a guided tour of the basics. ## Use the prompt box The prompt box supports several features: * **Permission modes**: click the mode indicator at the bottom of the prompt box to switch modes. In normal mode, Claude asks permission before each action. In Plan mode, Claude describes what it will do and waits for approval before making changes. VS Code automatically opens the plan as a full markdown document where you can add inline comments to give feedback before Claude begins. In auto-accept mode, Claude makes edits without asking. Set the default in VS Code settings under `claudeCode.initialPermissionMode`. * **Command menu**: click `/` or type `/` to open the command menu. Options include attaching files, switching models, toggling extended thinking, and viewing plan usage (`/usage`). The Customize section provides access to MCP servers, hooks, memory, permissions, and plugins. Items with a terminal icon open in the integrated terminal. * **Context indicator**: the prompt box shows how much of Claude's context window you're using. Claude automatically compacts when needed, or you can run `/compact` manually. * **Extended thinking**: lets Claude spend more time reasoning through complex problems. Toggle it on via the command menu (`/`). See [Extended thinking](/en/common-workflows#use-extended-thinking-thinking-mode) for details. * **Multi-line input**: press `Shift+Enter` to add a new line without sending. This also works in the "Other" free-text input of question dialogs. ### Reference files and folders Use @-mentions to give Claude context about specific files or folders. When you type `@` followed by a file or folder name, Claude reads that content and can answer questions about it or make changes to it. Claude Code supports fuzzy matching, so you can type partial names to find what you need: ```text theme={null} > Explain the logic in @auth (fuzzy matches auth.js, AuthService.ts, etc.) > What's in @src/components/ (include a trailing slash for folders) ``` For large PDFs, you can ask Claude to read specific pages instead of the whole file: a single page, a range like pages 1-10, or an open-ended range like page 3 onward. When you select text in the editor, Claude can see your highlighted code automatically. The prompt box footer shows how many lines are selected. Press `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) to insert an @-mention with the file path and line numbers (e.g., `@app.ts#5-10`). Click the selection indicator to toggle whether Claude can see your highlighted text - the eye-slash icon means the selection is hidden from Claude. You can also hold `Shift` while dragging files into the prompt box to add them as attachments. Click the X on any attachment to remove it from context. ### Resume past conversations Click the dropdown at the top of the Claude Code panel to access your conversation history. You can search by keyword or browse by time (Today, Yesterday, Last 7 days, etc.). Click any conversation to resume it with the full message history. Hover over a session to reveal rename and remove actions: rename to give it a descriptive title, or remove to delete it from the list. For more on resuming sessions, see [Common workflows](/en/common-workflows#resume-previous-conversations). ### Resume remote sessions from Claude.ai If you use [Claude Code on the web](/en/claude-code-on-the-web), you can resume those remote sessions directly in VS Code. This requires signing in with **Claude.ai Subscription**, not Anthropic Console. Click the **Past Conversations** dropdown at the top of the Claude Code panel. The dialog shows two tabs: Local and Remote. Click **Remote** to see sessions from claude.ai. Browse or search your remote sessions. Click any session to download it and continue the conversation locally. Only web sessions started with a GitHub repository appear in the Remote tab. Resuming loads the conversation history locally; changes are not synced back to claude.ai. ## Customize your workflow Once you're up and running, you can reposition the Claude panel, run multiple sessions, or switch to terminal mode. ### Choose where Claude lives You can drag the Claude panel to reposition it anywhere in VS Code. Grab the panel's tab or title bar and drag it to: * **Secondary sidebar**: the right side of the window. Keeps Claude visible while you code. * **Primary sidebar**: the left sidebar with icons for Explorer, Search, etc. * **Editor area**: opens Claude as a tab alongside your files. Useful for side tasks. Use the sidebar for your main Claude session and open additional tabs for side tasks. Claude remembers your preferred location. The Activity Bar sessions list icon is separate from the Claude panel: the sessions list is always visible in the Activity Bar, while the Claude panel icon only appears there when the panel is docked to the left sidebar. ### Run multiple conversations Use **Open in New Tab** or **Open in New Window** from the Command Palette to start additional conversations. Each conversation maintains its own history and context, allowing you to work on different tasks in parallel. When using tabs, a small colored dot on the spark icon indicates status: blue means a permission request is pending, orange means Claude finished while the tab was hidden. ### Switch to terminal mode By default, the extension opens a graphical chat panel. If you prefer the CLI-style interface, open the [Use Terminal setting](vscode://settings/claudeCode.useTerminal) and check the box. You can also open VS Code settings (`Cmd+,` on Mac or `Ctrl+,` on Windows/Linux), go to Extensions → Claude Code, and check **Use Terminal**. ## Manage plugins The VS Code extension includes a graphical interface for installing and managing [plugins](/en/plugins). Type `/plugins` in the prompt box to open the **Manage plugins** interface. ### Install plugins The plugin dialog shows two tabs: **Plugins** and **Marketplaces**. In the Plugins tab: * **Installed plugins** appear at the top with toggle switches to enable or disable them * **Available plugins** from your configured marketplaces appear below * Search to filter plugins by name or description * Click **Install** on any available plugin When you install a plugin, choose the installation scope: * **Install for you**: available in all your projects (user scope) * **Install for this project**: shared with project collaborators (project scope) * **Install locally**: only for you, only in this repository (local scope) ### Manage marketplaces Switch to the **Marketplaces** tab to add or remove plugin sources: * Enter a GitHub repo, URL, or local path to add a new marketplace * Click the refresh icon to update a marketplace's plugin list * Click the trash icon to remove a marketplace After making changes, a banner prompts you to restart Claude Code to apply the updates. Plugin management in VS Code uses the same CLI commands under the hood. Plugins and marketplaces you configure in the extension are also available in the CLI, and vice versa. For more about the plugin system, see [Plugins](/en/plugins) and [Plugin marketplaces](/en/plugin-marketplaces). ## Automate browser tasks with Chrome Connect Claude to your Chrome browser to test web apps, debug with console logs, and automate browser workflows without leaving VS Code. This requires the [Claude in Chrome extension](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/claude/fcoeoabgfenejglbffodgkkbkcdhcgfn) version 1.0.36 or higher. Type `@browser` in the prompt box followed by what you want Claude to do: ```text theme={null} @browser go to localhost:3000 and check the console for errors ``` You can also open the attachment menu to select specific browser tools like opening a new tab or reading page content. Claude opens new tabs for browser tasks and shares your browser's login state, so it can access any site you're already signed into. For setup instructions, the full list of capabilities, and troubleshooting, see [Use Claude Code with Chrome](/en/chrome). ## VS Code commands and shortcuts Open the Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P` on Mac or `Ctrl+Shift+P` on Windows/Linux) and type "Claude Code" to see all available VS Code commands for the Claude Code extension. Some shortcuts depend on which panel is "focused" (receiving keyboard input). When your cursor is in a code file, the editor is focused. When your cursor is in Claude's prompt box, Claude is focused. Use `Cmd+Esc` / `Ctrl+Esc` to toggle between them. These are VS Code commands for controlling the extension. Not all built-in Claude Code commands are available in the extension. See [VS Code extension vs. Claude Code CLI](#vs-code-extension-vs-claude-code-cli) for details. | Command | Shortcut | Description | | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | Focus Input | `Cmd+Esc` (Mac) / `Ctrl+Esc` (Windows/Linux) | Toggle focus between editor and Claude | | Open in Side Bar | - | Open Claude in the left sidebar | | Open in Terminal | - | Open Claude in terminal mode | | Open in New Tab | `Cmd+Shift+Esc` (Mac) / `Ctrl+Shift+Esc` (Windows/Linux) | Open a new conversation as an editor tab | | Open in New Window | - | Open a new conversation in a separate window | | New Conversation | `Cmd+N` (Mac) / `Ctrl+N` (Windows/Linux) | Start a new conversation (requires Claude to be focused) | | Insert @-Mention Reference | `Option+K` (Mac) / `Alt+K` (Windows/Linux) | Insert a reference to the current file and selection (requires editor to be focused) | | Show Logs | - | View extension debug logs | | Logout | - | Sign out of your Anthropic account | ## Configure settings The extension has two types of settings: * **Extension settings** in VS Code: control the extension's behavior within VS Code. Open with `Cmd+,` (Mac) or `Ctrl+,` (Windows/Linux), then go to Extensions → Claude Code. You can also type `/` and select **General Config** to open settings. * **Claude Code settings** in `~/.claude/settings.json`: shared between the extension and CLI. Use for allowed commands, environment variables, hooks, and MCP servers. See [Settings](/en/settings) for details. Add `"$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json"` to your `settings.json` to get autocomplete and inline validation for all available settings directly in VS Code. ### Extension settings | Setting | Default | Description | | --------------------------------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `selectedModel` | `default` | Model for new conversations. Change per-session with `/model`. | | `useTerminal` | `false` | Launch Claude in terminal mode instead of graphical panel | | `initialPermissionMode` | `default` | Controls approval prompts: `default` (ask each time), `plan`, `acceptEdits`, or `bypassPermissions` | | `preferredLocation` | `panel` | Where Claude opens: `sidebar` (right) or `panel` (new tab) | | `autosave` | `true` | Auto-save files before Claude reads or writes them | | `useCtrlEnterToSend` | `false` | Use Ctrl/Cmd+Enter instead of Enter to send prompts | | `enableNewConversationShortcut` | `true` | Enable Cmd/Ctrl+N to start a new conversation | | `hideOnboarding` | `false` | Hide the onboarding checklist (graduation cap icon) | | `respectGitIgnore` | `true` | Exclude .gitignore patterns from file searches | | `environmentVariables` | `[]` | Set environment variables for the Claude process. Use Claude Code settings instead for shared config. | | `disableLoginPrompt` | `false` | Skip authentication prompts (for third-party provider setups) | | `allowDangerouslySkipPermissions` | `false` | Bypass all permission prompts. **Use with extreme caution.** | | `claudeProcessWrapper` | - | Executable path used to launch the Claude process | ## VS Code extension vs. Claude Code CLI Claude Code is available as both a VS Code extension (graphical panel) and a CLI (command-line interface in the terminal). Some features are only available in the CLI. If you need a CLI-only feature, run `claude` in VS Code's integrated terminal. | Feature | CLI | VS Code Extension | | ------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | Commands and skills | [All](/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands) | Subset (type `/` to see available) | | MCP server config | Yes | Partial (add servers via CLI; manage existing servers with `/mcp` in the chat panel) | | Checkpoints | Yes | Yes | | `!` bash shortcut | Yes | No | | Tab completion | Yes | No | ### Rewind with checkpoints The VS Code extension supports checkpoints, which track Claude's file edits and let you rewind to a previous state. Hover over any message to reveal the rewind button, then choose from three options: * **Fork conversation from here**: start a new conversation branch from this message while keeping all code changes intact * **Rewind code to here**: revert file changes back to this point in the conversation while keeping the full conversation history * **Fork conversation and rewind code**: start a new conversation branch and revert file changes to this point For full details on how checkpoints work and their limitations, see [Checkpointing](/en/checkpointing). ### Run CLI in VS Code To use the CLI while staying in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (`` Ctrl+` `` on Windows/Linux or `` Cmd+` `` on Mac) and run `claude`. The CLI automatically integrates with your IDE for features like diff viewing and diagnostic sharing. If using an external terminal, run `/ide` inside Claude Code to connect it to VS Code. ### Switch between extension and CLI The extension and CLI share the same conversation history. To continue an extension conversation in the CLI, run `claude --resume` in the terminal. This opens an interactive picker where you can search for and select your conversation. ### Include terminal output in prompts Reference terminal output in your prompts using `@terminal:name` where `name` is the terminal's title. This lets Claude see command output, error messages, or logs without copy-pasting. ### Monitor background processes When Claude runs long-running commands, the extension shows progress in the status bar. However, visibility for background tasks is limited compared to the CLI. For better visibility, have Claude output the command so you can run it in VS Code's integrated terminal. ### Connect to external tools with MCP MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers give Claude access to external tools, databases, and APIs. To add an MCP server, open the integrated terminal (`` Ctrl+` `` or `` Cmd+` ``) and run: ```bash theme={null} claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ ``` Once configured, ask Claude to use the tools (e.g., "Review PR #456"). To manage MCP servers without leaving VS Code, type `/mcp` in the chat panel. The MCP management dialog lets you enable or disable servers, reconnect to a server, and manage OAuth authentication. See the [MCP documentation](/en/mcp) for available servers. ## Work with git Claude Code integrates with git to help with version control workflows directly in VS Code. Ask Claude to commit changes, create pull requests, or work across branches. ### Create commits and pull requests Claude can stage changes, write commit messages, and create pull requests based on your work: ```text theme={null} > commit my changes with a descriptive message > create a pr for this feature > summarize the changes I've made to the auth module ``` When creating pull requests, Claude generates descriptions based on the actual code changes and can add context about testing or implementation decisions. ### Use git worktrees for parallel tasks Use the `--worktree` (`-w`) flag to start Claude in an isolated worktree with its own files and branch: ```bash theme={null} claude --worktree feature-auth ``` Each worktree maintains independent file state while sharing git history. This prevents Claude instances from interfering with each other when working on different tasks. For more details, see [Run parallel sessions with Git worktrees](/en/common-workflows#run-parallel-claude-code-sessions-with-git-worktrees). ## Use third-party providers By default, Claude Code connects directly to Anthropic's API. If your organization uses Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry to access Claude, configure the extension to use your provider instead: Open the [Disable Login Prompt setting](vscode://settings/claudeCode.disableLoginPrompt) and check the box. You can also open VS Code settings (`Cmd+,` on Mac or `Ctrl+,` on Windows/Linux), search for "Claude Code login", and check **Disable Login Prompt**. Follow the setup guide for your provider: * [Claude Code on Amazon Bedrock](/en/amazon-bedrock) * [Claude Code on Google Vertex AI](/en/google-vertex-ai) * [Claude Code on Microsoft Foundry](/en/microsoft-foundry) These guides cover configuring your provider in `~/.claude/settings.json`, which ensures your settings are shared between the VS Code extension and the CLI. ## Security and privacy Your code stays private. Claude Code processes your code to provide assistance but does not use it to train models. For details on data handling and how to opt out of logging, see [Data and privacy](/en/data-usage). With auto-edit permissions enabled, Claude Code can modify VS Code configuration files (like `settings.json` or `tasks.json`) that VS Code may execute automatically. To reduce risk when working with untrusted code: * Enable [VS Code Restricted Mode](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/workspace-trust#_restricted-mode) for untrusted workspaces * Use manual approval mode instead of auto-accept for edits * Review changes carefully before accepting them ## Fix common issues ### Extension won't install * Ensure you have a compatible version of VS Code (1.98.0 or later) * Check that VS Code has permission to install extensions * Try installing directly from the [VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anthropic.claude-code) ### Spark icon not visible The Spark icon appears in the **Editor Toolbar** (top-right of editor) when you have a file open. If you don't see it: 1. **Open a file**: The icon requires a file to be open. Having just a folder open isn't enough. 2. **Check VS Code version**: Requires 1.98.0 or higher (Help → About) 3. **Restart VS Code**: Run "Developer: Reload Window" from the Command Palette 4. **Disable conflicting extensions**: Temporarily disable other AI extensions (Cline, Continue, etc.) 5. **Check workspace trust**: The extension doesn't work in Restricted Mode Alternatively, click "✱ Claude Code" in the **Status Bar** (bottom-right corner). This works even without a file open. You can also use the **Command Palette** (`Cmd+Shift+P` / `Ctrl+Shift+P`) and type "Claude Code". ### Claude Code never responds If Claude Code isn't responding to your prompts: 1. **Check your internet connection**: Ensure you have a stable internet connection 2. **Start a new conversation**: Try starting a fresh conversation to see if the issue persists 3. **Try the CLI**: Run `claude` from the terminal to see if you get more detailed error messages If problems persist, [file an issue on GitHub](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues) with details about the error. ## Uninstall the extension To uninstall the Claude Code extension: 1. Open the Extensions view (`Cmd+Shift+X` on Mac or `Ctrl+Shift+X` on Windows/Linux) 2. Search for "Claude Code" 3. Click **Uninstall** To also remove extension data and reset all settings: ```bash theme={null} rm -rf ~/.vscode/globalStorage/anthropic.claude-code ``` For additional help, see the [troubleshooting guide](/en/troubleshooting). ## Next steps Now that you have Claude Code set up in VS Code: * [Explore common workflows](/en/common-workflows) to get the most out of Claude Code * [Set up MCP servers](/en/mcp) to extend Claude's capabilities with external tools. Add servers using the CLI, then manage them with `/mcp` in the chat panel. * [Configure Claude Code settings](/en/settings) to customize allowed commands, hooks, and more. These settings are shared between the extension and CLI.

content.thinking:
Okay so the key finding here is: Claude Code's VS Code extension does NOT have the ability for Claude to programmatically open files in editor tabs. The extension gives Claude tools like Read, Edit, Write, Bash - but not "open this file as a VS Code tab." The `code --goto` command works from the terminal, but Claude Code running inside the extension doesn't have a VS Code API tool to manipulate the editor UI.

However - Claude Code CAN run bash commands. So it could run `code --goto file.md` from bash. Let me think about whether that would work when running inside VS Code's integrated terminal...

Actually, the `code` CLI command should work from within the integrated terminal. 

So here's the thing: running `code file.md` from the integrated terminal opens that file as a tab in the same window, which means Claude Code could use the Bash tool to open files this way regardless of whether it's running in extension mode or terminal mode.

The `code` CLI command is available within VS Code, so Claude Code can invoke it via Bash to open files as tabs. For markdown preview specifically, VS Code has a built-in command, but triggering it from the CLI isn't straightforward—that would really need a small VS Code extension to expose the command properly. So the practical approach here is using the `code` command to open files, which works reliably, rather than trying to trigger specific VS Code commands from the CLI.

content.text:
Whoever told you that was *mostly* right — but there's a workaround that changes the picture.

Claude Code's toolset (Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep) doesn't include anything like "OpenTab" or "ShowPreview." There's no VS Code API tool. So Claude can't *natively* manipulate the editor UI. That part is true.

**But Claude Code has Bash.** And when Claude Code runs inside VS Code — either in the integrated terminal or via the extension — the `code` CLI is available. So Claude can do:

```bash
code --reuse-window .context/PROFILE.md
code --reuse-window .context/MEMORY.md
code --reuse-window src/auth/concern.ts
```

Each of those opens as a tab in the current VS Code window. It's indirect — going through the shell rather than the VS Code API — but it works. A `/touch-full-domain` skill could read the canonical files, brief you in the chat, *and* open them as tabs. During the session, when Claude discovers a file of concern, it runs `code path/to/file` and it appears.

**The limitation is markdown preview.** You can open a `.md` file as a tab, but triggering VS Code's "Markdown: Open Preview to the Side" (`Ctrl+Shift+V`) isn't something you can do from the `code` CLI. The user has to hit that shortcut themselves, or you'd need a tiny VS Code extension that exposes a command like `code --command markdown.showPreviewToSide`. That's maybe 20 lines of TypeScript to write as a VS Code extension — register a URI handler or a command that opens a file and triggers the preview.

**For session logging**, the VS Code extension has a `claudeProcessWrapper` setting — an executable path that wraps the Claude process. That's literally your wrapper hook for session logging. You point it at a script that tees the session output to a log file in `.context/sessions/` while passing everything through to Claude normally.

So the realistic stack is:

- **VS Code** as the viewport (tabs, splits, markdown preview, file explorer)
- **Claude Code extension** for the chat interface, with skills for touch/checkpoint
- **`code` CLI via Bash** for Claude to surface files as tabs during a session
- **SessionStart hook** to auto-open domain context files when entering a domain
- **`claudeProcessWrapper`** for session logging (feeds the distiller)
- **A tiny VS Code extension** (optional) if you want Claude to trigger markdown preview programmatically
- **Headless `claude -p`** for the distiller, running in isolated context

The one thing you'd want to verify is whether `code` CLI commands work reliably from within the extension panel's Bash tool versus the integrated terminal. If there's friction, you just set `useTerminal: true` and run Claude Code in terminal mode inside VS Code. You lose the graphical diff view but gain reliable shell access.

Want me to sketch out what the domain-entry hook and the touch skill would actually look like in this setup?

Sources:
- [Claude Code VS Code Integration](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/vs-code)
- [Claude Code CLI Reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference)
- [Claude Code Hooks Reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

Based on the search results, here's what I found about auto-running tasks in VS Code workspaces:

## Key Information

**Auto-run Tasks on Workspace Open:**

To configure automatic tasks in a `.code-workspace` file, you can add a tasks section with `"runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }`, which will run the task when the workspace opens.

**Task Configuration:**

The `runOptions` with `"runOn": "folderOpen"` enables running the command when the folder opens, and the `presentation` settings control whether a new terminal panel is revealed.

**Permission Setup:**

When the launch task completes, end it with Ctrl + C and you will receive a popup message asking for permission to run the task on open of the folder—select "Allow and run".

**Opening with `code --new-window`:**

The search results indicate that you can launch VS Code from a terminal by passing the path to a folder as the first argument to the code command, and the auto-run task configuration will apply when that workspace opens.

The `--new-window` flag will open the workspace in a new window, and any tasks configured with `"runOn": "folderOpen"` in either the `.vscode/tasks.json` or `.code-workspace` file will automatically execute when the workspace loads.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about running terminal commands automatically when opening a VS Code workspace folder:

## Setting Up Automatic Task Execution on Folder Open

The tasks specific to your project are stored in `<project folder>/.vscode/tasks.json`. You can create the file yourself, or run the Tasks: Configure Task command to build a template file for you.

### Basic Configuration

To automatically run a command when you open a folder, add a task with `"runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }`. Here's an example structure:

A shell task might look like: `{ "label": "Run npm webpack on startup", "type": "shell", "command": "npm run webpack", "windows": { "command": "npm run webpack" }, "presentation": { "reveal": "always", "panel": "new" }, "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" } }`

### Key Configuration Options

- **type**: Use `"type": "shell"` to indicate this is a terminal command task
- **command**: The `command` property tells VS Code what command to run
- **presentation**: The behavior of the terminal can be controlled using the presentation property of a task, with the reveal property controlling whether the Integrated Terminal panel is brought to front
- **runOptions**: Setting `"folderOpen"` will run the task when the containing folder is opened

### Enabling Automatic Tasks

You can enable automatic tasks by running the command Tasks: Allow Automatic Tasks in Folder. The first time you open a project that contains a task that runs on "folderOpen", you should get a prompt asking if you want to allow tasks to run automatically in that folder.

### For npm Projects

For npm scripts, you can use: `{ "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "type": "npm", "script": "dev", "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" } } ] }`


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
This is it. The whole thing clicks together with workspace files.

Here's the play: each domain gets a `.code-workspace` file — either generated by `/touch-full-domain` or committed alongside the domain. The workspace file does three things:

**1. Sets the folders** (what VS Code opens):
```json
{
  "folders": [
    { "path": "." },
    { "path": ".context", "name": "Domain Context" }
  ]
}
```

**2. Auto-opens the context files** via a `folderOpen` task that runs `code` to open PROFILE.md and whatever else matters:
```json
{
  "tasks": {
    "version": "2.0.0",
    "tasks": [
      {
        "label": "Open Domain Context",
        "type": "shell",
        "command": "code .context/PROFILE.md .context/STATE.md",
        "presentation": { "reveal": "silent" },
        "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }
      },
      {
        "label": "Start Domain Session",
        "type": "shell",
        "command": "claude --append-system-prompt-file .context/agent.md 'Run /touch-full-domain and brief me'",
        "presentation": { "reveal": "always", "panel": "new" },
        "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

**3. Workspace settings** for this domain — extensions to activate, markdown preview behavior, whatever you need per-domain.

Then your entry point from *anywhere* — terminal, script, another Claude session, Cowork — is one line:

```bash
code --new-window /path/to/domain/domain.code-workspace
```

New VS Code window opens. Context files appear as tabs. Claude Code launches in the terminal with the domain's agent prompt and runs touch. You're in discourse. No manual setup.

**What `/touch-full-domain` can now do:**
- Validate `.context/` structure
- Regenerate PROFILE.md
- Generate or update the `.code-workspace` file with current files of concern
- The workspace file becomes a living artifact — touch updates it based on what matters *now*

**The firehose sweep becomes:**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# firehose-sweep.sh - reads REGISTRY.md, opens a window per pending domain
while IFS='|' read -r domain path status _; do
  if [[ "$status" =~ "pending" ]]; then
    code --new-window "${path}/domain.code-workspace"
  fi
done < <(tail -n +3 firehose/REGISTRY.md)
```

Or more controlled — one at a time, marking active as you go.

**Session logging:** the `claudeProcessWrapper` VS Code setting points at a script that tees output to `.context/sessions/`. Or a `Stop` hook writes the session summary. Either way, the distiller has its input.

**The distiller** runs headless after you close the window:
```bash
claude -p --system-prompt-file distiller-prompt.md \
       "Distill domain at /path/to/domain"
```

Isolated context. No completion bias. Reads cold artifacts off disk.

So the stack is: a `.code-workspace` file per domain (generated/maintained by touch), the `code` CLI to instantiate viewports, Claude Code with hooks and skills for the session work, and headless `claude -p` for distillation. No tmux. No Lua. No custom extensions. Everything is either a VS Code workspace feature or a Claude Code primitive.

Want me to start building this? I could draft the workspace file template and update the touch skill to generate it.

Sources:
- [VS Code Workspaces](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editing/workspaces/workspaces)
- [VS Code Tasks - Integrate External Tools](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/debugtest/tasks)
- [Auto-run tasks on folder open](https://www.roboleary.net/vscode/2020/10/19/vscode-task-onstartup.html)
- [Auto-open terminals in VS Code workspace](https://jackharner.com/blog/auto-open-terminals-vs-code-workspace/)

content.content:
1→# Domain Orchestrator Architecture
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→The orchestrator manages a constellation of **domains** — folders representing concerns of varying scope and lifespan. It provides tools to bootstrap, navigate, sweep, and monitor domains through a consistent interface.
     6→
     7→The architecture is two-tier:
     8→
     9→- **Orchestrator**: long-lived, holds the domain registry, tracks sweep progress, manages subagent lifecycle
    10→- **Subagents**: ephemeral per-domain, clean context, communicate through the filesystem via the `.context/` convention
    11→
    12→The disk is the shared bus. Subagents do not talk to each other or to the orchestrator in memory. They read from and write to the domain's `.context/` directory. This ensures clean context isolation and makes the system interruptible and resumable by design.
    13→
    14→## Domain Registry
    15→
    16→The orchestrator maintains a **registry** — a map of all known domains. This is the input to every orchestrator command. The registry contains:
    17→
    18→- Domain path (folder location)
    19→- Domain name / identifier
    20→- Last touched timestamp
    21→- Structural health (does `.context/` exist and validate?)
    22→- Profile currency (when was PROFILE.md last generated?)
    23→
    24→The registry is a file on disk (format TBD — likely markdown or YAML). It is the single source of truth for what domains exist and their orchestrator-level metadata.
    25→
    26→## Commands
    27→
    28→### `/touch`
    29→
    30→Lightweight structural validation across one or more domains.
    31→
    32→**What it does:**
    33→- Validates `.context/` directory exists
    34→- Checks for required files: agent.md, STATE.md, MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md
    35→- Validates pointer integrity in agent.md (do referenced files exist?)
    36→- Reports structural health
    37→
    38→**Characteristics:**
    39→- Fast, cheap, parallelizable
    40→- No content reading or synthesis
    41→- Idempotent — can create missing scaffolding or just validate
    42→- Can run across the entire registry as a preflight check
    43→
    44→**Usage:**
    45→```
    46→/touch                     # touch all domains in registry
    47→/touch <domain>            # touch a single domain
    48→```
    49→
    50→### `/touch --full`
    51→
    52→Full bootstrap with profile generation.
    53→
    54→**What it does:**
    55→- Everything `/touch` does, plus:
    56→- Spins up a subagent context per domain
    57→- Reads README.md, STATE.md, agent.md, MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md
    58→- Scans the domain's repo/folder contents
    59→- Generates or updates PROFILE.md
    60→
    61→**Characteristics:**
    62→- Expensive — requires a model call per domain
    63→- Parallelizable with configurable concurrency limits
    64→- Each domain gets a clean subagent context (no cross-domain bleed)
    65→
    66→**Usage:**
    67→```
    68→/touch --full              # full touch all domains
    69→/touch --full <domain>     # full touch a single domain
    70→```
    71→
    72→### `/map`
    73→
    74→Display and manage the domain registry.
    75→
    76→**What it does:**
    77→- Shows the registry as a navigable text view
    78→- Displays structural health, profile currency, last-touched timestamps
    79→- Supports filtering, sorting, grouping
    80→- Allows adding/removing/reorganizing domains
    81→
    82→**Characteristics:**
    83→- Read-only by default (display mode)
    84→- Can be combined with touch as a preflight: `/map` after `/touch` shows current health
    85→
    86→**Usage:**
    87→```
    88→/map                       # display full registry
    89→/map --stale               # show domains with outdated profiles
    90→/map --add <path>          # register a new domain
    91→/map --remove <domain>     # unregister a domain
    92→```
    93→
    94→### `/firehose`
    95→
    96→Serial sweep through domains with interactive review.
    97→
    98→**What it does:**
    99→1. Reads the registry, determines sweep order
   100→2. For each domain, sequentially:
   101→   a. Starts a clean subagent context
   102→   b. Ingests PROFILE.md (or runs touch --full if stale)
   103→   c. Scans the repo, updates profile currency
   104→   d. Produces a **briefing document** — written to disk, openable in editor
   105→   e. Enters interactive phase: gap identification, decision points, questions
   106→   f. Human decides or defers on each identified issue
   107→   g. `/checkpoint` captures progress during the session
   108→   h. `/checkpoint --close` ends the domain's session
   109→3. Moves to next domain
   110→
   111→**The briefing document** is both the subagent's deliverable and the session's resumption point. It contains:
   112→- At-a-glance domain status
   113→- Outstanding issues and blockers
   114→- Identified gaps (missing context, stale decisions, unresolved threads)
   115→- Decision points with evident order of consequence
   116→- Recommendations where the agent has enough context
   117→
   118→**Characteristics:**
   119→- Serial by design — one domain at a time, full attention
   120→- Interruptible between domains (sweep state tracked on disk)
   121→- Interruptible within a domain (checkpoints preserve progress)
   122→- Resumable — re-entering firehose picks up where you left off
   123→- Each domain gets a clean subagent context
   124→
   125→**Sweep state** is tracked in a file:
   126→```
   127→sweep/
   128→  <sweep-id>.md
   129→    - Started: <timestamp>
   130→    - Domains: [list with status: pending | active | complete | deferred]
   131→    - Current: <domain>
   132→    - Phase: scanning | briefing | interactive | resolved
   133→```
   134→
   135→**Usage:**
   136→```
   137→/firehose                  # start or resume a sweep
   138→/firehose --new            # start a fresh sweep
   139→/firehose --skip           # skip current domain, move to next
   140→/firehose --defer          # defer current domain, move to next
   141→```
   142→
   143→### `/checkpoint`
   144→
   145→Signal the agent to capture a structured snapshot of the current session state.
   146→
   147→**What it does:**
   148→- Agent writes a checkpoint entry to the session file
   149→- Captures: what happened since last checkpoint, decisions made, open questions, current thinking
   150→
   151→**Characteristics:**
   152→- Human-initiated (agent may suggest, but never checkpoints without consent)
   153→- Cheap — minimal interruption to workflow
   154→- Checkpoints are the **unit of work** for distillation
   155→- `/checkpoint --close` signals session end and triggers memory draft
   156→
   157→**Usage:**
   158→```
   159→/checkpoint                # capture current state
   160→/checkpoint --close        # close session, write memory draft
   161→```
   162→
   163→## Domain Viewport
   164→
   165→The four-pane view for engaging with a domain:
   166→
   167→| Pane | Purpose | Temporal Character |
   168→|------|---------|-------------------|
   169→| PROFILE.md | Static briefing — orient before engaging | Derived, regenerated |
   170→| MEMORY.md | Accumulated understanding across visits | Grows over time, distilled |
   171→| DECISIONS.md | Decision record with rationale | Append-only, structured |
   172→| Agent Chat | Live workspace — what we're doing now | Ephemeral (checkpointed) |
   173→
   174→**Read direction** (entering a domain): Profile → Memory → Decisions → Chat
   175→**Write direction** (leaving a domain): Chat → Checkpoints → Distiller → Memory + Decisions → Profile (next touch --full)
   176→
   177→## Subagent Lifecycle
   178→
   179→1. **Spawn**: Orchestrator launches a subagent with a scoped prompt, pointed at the domain root
   180→2. **Bootstrap**: Subagent reads agent.md (start file), loads context per the context map
   181→3. **Execute**: Subagent performs its task (touch --full scan, firehose interactive session, etc.)
   182→4. **Checkpoint**: Structured snapshots written to `.context/sessions/` during execution
   183→5. **Close**: Session ends, memory draft written, subagent terminates
   184→6. **Distill**: Post-session distillation pipeline processes session artifacts (separate process)
   185→
   186→## Concurrency
   187→
   188→- `/touch` (light): highly parallelizable, no model calls, limited only by filesystem
   189→- `/touch --full`: parallelizable with configurable concurrency cap (N simultaneous subagents)
   190→- `/firehose`: serial by design
   191→- Distillation: can run in background, async, or batched — no contention with interactive sessions
   192→
   193→Concurrency limits are configurable at the orchestrator level (not per-domain).
   194→
   195→## Real-Time Monitoring
   196→
   197→For parallel operations (touch --full across many domains), the orchestrator should provide:
   198→
   199→- Visibility into what subagents are doing
   200→- Ability to cancel individual subagents
   201→- Status updates as domains complete
   202→
   203→Implementation: background tmux sessions write status to a shared file. In neovim, a dedicated buffer or statusline component displays sweep progress. For deeper inspection, open a terminal buffer attached to a specific subagent's tmux session.
   204→
   205→## Persistence and Resumability
   206→
   207→All orchestrator state is disk-backed:
   208→
   209→- **Registry**: domain list and metadata
   210→- **Sweep state**: firehose progress (which domains done, which pending, current phase)
   211→- **Session state**: checkpoints within a domain session
   212→- **Canonical context**: MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md, PROFILE.md
   213→
   214→Nothing critical lives only in memory. Any process can be interrupted (ctrl-c, crash, "I'm going to lunch") and resumed from the last checkpoint or sweep position.
   215→
   216→## Runtime Environment
   217→
   218→### Environment
   219→
   220→| Layer | Tool | Role |
   221→|-------|------|------|
   222→| Editor / body | Neovim (LazyVim) | Buffers, tabs, splits, domain navigation, markdown rendering |
   223→| Process manager | tmux | Subagent lifecycle, session persistence, background jobs |
   224→| Desktop | Linux Mint, MATE, X11 | Host environment |
   225→
   226→The orchestrator runs as local user. No containers, no remote execution.
   227→
   228→### Subagent Runtime
   229→
   230→Subagents are `claude` CLI invocations running inside tmux sessions. The orchestrator script creates and manages tmux sessions. Neovim attaches to them via terminal buffers when interactive access is needed.
   231→
   232→### Neovim as the Body
   233→
   234→Neovim is the primary workspace. Three modes of use:
   235→
   236→**Editing mode.** Standard neovim buffers. `.context/` files open as editable splits with Treesitter markdown highlighting, folding, and telescope search across all domains. This is where you read PROFILE.md, edit MEMORY.md, review DECISIONS.md.
   237→
   238→**Session mode.** A neovim tab containing a terminal buffer attached to a tmux session running Claude Code. This is the firehose interactive session — the agent chat. Context files can be open in splits alongside the terminal buffer, or in adjacent tabs.
   239→
   240→**Background mode.** tmux sessions running without a neovim tab attached. Parallel touch --full, distillation, any non-interactive subagent work. You can open a neovim terminal buffer to check on them at any time, or let them write to disk and review later.
   241→
   242→### Domain Viewport in Neovim
   243→
   244→Each domain gets a **tab** in neovim. The tab contains:
   245→
   246→- Terminal buffer attached to the domain's tmux session (Claude Code firehose)
   247→- PROFILE.md, MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md as editable splits
   248→
   249→```
   250→┌─────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┐
   251→│                                     │  PROFILE.md              │
   252→│                                     │  (editable buffer)       │
   253→│   Terminal buffer                   ├──────────────────────────┤
   254→│   (tmux: firehose-domain-a)        │  MEMORY.md               │
   255→│                                     │  (editable buffer)       │
   256→│                                     ├──────────────────────────┤
   257→│                                     │  DECISIONS.md            │
   258→│                                     │  (editable buffer)       │
   259→└─────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┘
   260→```
   261→
   262→The neovim tab bar is the sweep history. Completed domains stay as tabs you can flip back to. Each tab is a fresh layout — no buffer reuse across domains.
   263→
   264→An orchestrator lua module provides domain-aware commands:
   265→
   266→```lua
   267→-- Open a domain viewport as a new tab
   268→function open_domain(domain_path)
   269→  vim.cmd('tabnew')
   270→  vim.cmd('edit ' .. domain_path .. '/.context/PROFILE.md')
   271→  vim.cmd('vsplit')
   272→  vim.cmd('terminal tmux attach -t firehose-' .. domain_name)
   273→  -- additional splits for MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md
   274→end
   275→```
   276→
   277→**Telescope integration.** `/map` can be implemented as a telescope picker — list all registered domains, preview their PROFILE.md, and open the full viewport on selection.
   278→
   279→### tmux Role (Background)
   280→
   281→tmux is not the UI layer. It provides:
   282→
   283→- **Session persistence**: claude CLI processes survive if neovim closes. Reopen neovim and reattach.
   284→- **Subagent isolation**: each domain's firehose session is a named tmux session (`firehose-<domain>`).
   285→- **Parallel execution**: touch --full and distillation jobs run as background tmux sessions.
   286→- **Collaborative access**: Stephanie can `tmux attach -t firehose-<domain>` from her own terminal to observe or participate.
   287→
   288→## Implementation Path
   289→
   290→**Phase 1 — Minimal Viable Orchestrator:**
   291→- Shell script wrapping `claude` CLI, run as local user
   292→- Domain registry as a text file
   293→- Sequential firehose loop — script creates tmux sessions, user attaches via neovim terminal buffers
   294→- `/touch` as structural validation (no model call)
   295→- `/checkpoint` as agent instruction in the system prompt
   296→- Manual neovim tab setup per domain
   297→
   298→**Phase 2 — Neovim Integration + Distillation:**
   299→- Lua orchestrator module: `open_domain()`, telescope-based `/map`
   300→- Automated tab layout creation per domain
   301→- Standalone distiller script, run as local user (see Distiller Spec)
   302→- Post-session hook to trigger distillation
   303→- Parallel `touch --full` with concurrency cap (background tmux sessions)
   304→
   305→**Phase 3 — Polish:**
   306→- Sweep status integration (neovim statusline or dedicated buffer)
   307→- Telescope search across all domains' `.context/` files
   308→- Sweep analytics (time per domain, decision velocity, etc.)
   309→- Keybindings for checkpoint, domain-next, domain-skip, domain-defer
   310→
