toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Domain Kit Engineering (working title)

## What this is

Infrastructure for durable human-agent collaboration. Not per-session, not per-task — persistent, across time, with memory and accountability.

A **domain** is any concern you manage — a project, a client, a system, a body of research. Each domain carries a **kit**: prompts, personas, context, state, memories, and a session-distillation loop that gives agents reasoning continuity and humans a feedback gate.

The system provides:

- **Normalized domain kits** across all your domains. Every concern follows the same convention, so any agent entering any domain knows what to read, what to write, and what not to touch.
- **Context and intention hygiene.** Different clients, different concerns, different agent personas — cleanly separated. No context leakage between domains.
- **Clarity and continuity per domain.** MEMORY accumulates. DECISIONS record rationale with revisit conditions. PROFILE synthesizes it all into a one-page briefing. Every session starts warm, not cold.
- **Session capture from inception.** Domain creation is a session. The onboarding conversation that defines a domain is itself the first session artifact — available to the distiller, part of the history.
- **Housekeeping and git awareness.** `touch-domain` is the universal health check: structural validation, git state, profile currency. Concerns are surfaced, not silently fixed.
- **A viewport for interactive work.** Each domain opens as its own VS Code window with context files visible and Claude Code at the center. The agent briefs you on entry, surfaces files of concern during the session, and captures checkpoints on exit.
- **A distillation loop for reasoning continuity.** Session artifacts are processed by an isolated distiller — a separate agent with no access to the working session's context, providing objective review. Proposed updates to canonical files go through a human review gate. Knowledge compounds across sessions.
- **Claude Code superpowers.** Orchestration of subagents for parallel work. Model selection per domain. Objective and adversarial review via isolated headless invocations. Hooks for deterministic automation.

## Why

You have many concerns. Each one has context that matters — decisions made, constraints discovered, preferences learned, work in progress. Without a system, that context lives in your head or scatters across chat logs. Every new agent session starts from zero. You re-explain. The agent re-discovers. Knowledge doesn't compound.

This system makes the domain kit the unit of collaboration. The agent reads the kit on entry and contributes to it on exit. The distiller ensures quality. Git provides the audit trail. Over time, each domain's kit becomes a genuinely useful knowledge base — not just a project folder.

The goal is to scan all your concerns, make plans, digest decisions, and maintain awareness — with agents that remember, across sessions that build on each other.

## User story

You open a terminal. You're in the orchestrator — Claude Code running in the system repo. You ask for a scan: `touch-domain --no-touchy` across the registry. The agent reports back: domain statuses, git states, stale profiles, unprocessed sessions, revisit conditions that look met. You see the landscape.

You pick a domain to work on. The agent opens a viewport: `code --new-window ~/domains/cashflow/domain.code-workspace`. A new VS Code window appears. PROFILE.md, MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md open as tabs. Claude Code launches in the extension panel, loads context via hooks, and briefs you: "Last session was March 3rd. STATE shows the reconciliation module is blocked on API access. DECISIONS has a revisit condition that appears met — the vendor shipped their v2 API last week. Three unprocessed sessions pending distillation."

You're in discourse. You work. The agent surfaces files as tabs when they become relevant. You hit decision points — the agent captures them. When you're done, you checkpoint and close. The session artifacts land in `.context/sessions/`.

Back in the orchestrator, you kick off distillation — a headless, isolated agent reads the session artifacts against the canonical files and proposes updates. You review the diff. Approve. MEMORY and DECISIONS grow. The domain is smarter for next time.

Or you want to start something new. `touch-domain --new ~/domains/new-client`. "This path doesn't exist. Starting new domain onboarding." An interactive session begins — you define the domain, its scope, the agent persona, initial concerns. That conversation is captured as the first session. Git initializes. The bare repo appears on your server. The workspace file generates. The viewport opens. The domain is born, version-controlled, context-aware, and ready.

## How it works

See the design documents:

- `orchestrator-architecture.md` — commands, viewport, runtime, hooks, implementation path
- `domain-convention.md` — domain layout, file roles, git convention, lifecycle
- `distiller-spec.md` — distillation pipeline, isolation requirement, strategies, review gate

## Current state

Spec-revised, scope expanding. See `.context/STATE.md` for details.

- **Deciding factors**: Agent teams share a working directory and task list — designed for multiple agents on ONE problem, not isolation between domains. Messaging system would allow domain context leakage. Agent teams are appropriate within a single domain (e.g., parallel research) but not as the firehose sweep mechanism.
- **Revisit if**: Agent teams gain per-teammate working directory isolation

## 2026-03-10: touch-domain as universal modal command
- **Context**: Touch was a health check. New requirement: touch should also be the entry point for domain creation. When you touch a path that doesn't exist, it should bootstrap a new domain. The bootstrapping conversation itself becomes the first session artifact.
- **Alternatives considered**: Separate `create-domain` command, manual scaffolding then touch
- **Deciding factors**: One command to rule them all. `touch-domain` inspects state and picks the right action: light validation, full profile regen, or new domain onboarding. Explicit flags (`--full`, `--new`) for when you want to force a mode. Default is smart — prompts for escalation when conditions warrant. Designed for extensibility: more `--options` will emerge.
- **Revisit if**: The modal behavior becomes confusing or unpredictable; flags proliferate beyond usability

## 2026-03-10: Git-aware domains with standard remote conventions
- **Context**: Domains should be version-controlled by default. Git provides an audit trail for agentic changes, ensures work is not lost, and enables the bare-repo-on-server convention Richard uses across projects.
- **Alternatives considered**: No git integration (leave it to the user), per-domain opt-in only
- **Deciding factors**: Git is assumed for all domains unless explicitly exempted. Global defaults define the standard setup (bare repo on configured primary server, optional secondary mirror). Per-domain overrides in agent.md. Touch checks git status as part of housekeeping — surfaces concerns, doesn't silently fix. `touch-domain --new` initializes git as part of bootstrapping. Environment-specific settings (server hostnames, paths) live in `firehose.local.md`, not in the portable spec.
- **Revisit if**: Domains emerge that genuinely shouldn't be git repos (ephemeral scratch domains?); bare repo convention becomes impractical for some repo types

input.new_string:
- **Revisit if**: Domains emerge that genuinely shouldn't be git repos (ephemeral scratch domains?); bare repo convention becomes impractical for some repo types

## 2026-03-10: Command taxonomy — four concerns, four commands
- **Context**: Clarifying the command structure as the system scope expanded beyond the original "firehose" sweep concept. Need clean separation between kit management, viewport launching, session capture, and memory processing.
- **Alternatives considered**: Everything under `/firehose` umbrella, separate commands per function with inconsistent naming
- **Deciding factors**: Each command maps to a distinct concern and operational posture. `touch-domain` = objective kit management. `open-kit` = subjective viewport launch (with `--cursor`, `--terminal` as viewport targets). `checkpoint` = in-session capture. `distill` = isolated post-session processing. Clean separation means each can evolve independently. `touch-domain` and `distill` are objective (operate from outside). `checkpoint` is subjective (operates from inside). `open-kit` is the transition between them.
- **Revisit if**: Commands need to compose in ways the separation prevents; a fifth concern emerges that doesn't fit

## 2026-03-10: Firehose reframed as attention-direction (future feature)
- **Context**: "Firehose" was originally the name for the whole system and the serial sweep command. As the command taxonomy crystallized, firehose became one feature among several — and a different kind of feature: not kit management or viewport launching, but strategic scanning across all domains to surface what needs attention.
- **Alternatives considered**: Keep firehose as the system name, implement sweep immediately
- **Deciding factors**: The core commands (touch-domain, open-kit, checkpoint, distill) need to be proven in daily use before building the sweep. Firehose as attention-direction is a System 4 function (VSM) — intelligence, looking outward and forward. Different from System 2/3 (coordination/control, which is touch-domain) and System 1 (operations, which is open-kit + checkpoint). Design deferred until core is stable.
- **Revisit if**: The need for cross-domain scanning becomes urgent before core commands are proven

## 2026-03-10: Domain kit as foundational concept (variety-agent-design lineage)
- **Context**: The domain kit concept originates from cybernetics-grounded analysis of human-agent systems (Ashby's Law of Requisite Variety, Beer's Viable System Model). The term "domain kit" was coined in this project to describe the complete set of domain-specific resources a model is given access to — a viable system, not just a prompt.
- **Alternatives considered**: Using mainstream terminology ("context engineering", "agent skills", "prompt templates")
- **Deciding factors**: Existing terms don't capture the governance dimension — the session-distillation loop, variety engineering, completeness bias management, the domain kit as an ontological space. "Domain kit" does real work that existing vocabulary doesn't. The .context/ directory structure is a direct implementation of the domain kit concept: Configuration (agent.md, README.md) + State (MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md, STATE.md, sessions/).
- **Revisit if**: "Domain kit" collides with an established term in the field; a better term emerges

# Decisions — firehose

## 2026-03-09: Disk as shared bus (no in-memory subagent communication)
- **Context**: Designing subagent communication model for the orchestrator
- **Alternatives considered**: In-memory message passing, shared state object, RPC
- **Deciding factors**: Interruptibility and resumability by design; any process can crash and resume from last checkpoint; clean context isolation between domains
- **Revisit if**: Performance bottleneck from filesystem I/O becomes significant, or if real-time coordination between subagents is needed

## 2026-03-09: Serial firehose sweep (not parallel)
- **Context**: How to structure domain sweeps in `/firehose`
- **Alternatives considered**: Parallel domain processing
- **Deciding factors**: Full human attention per domain; interactive review phase requires focus; parallelism available for `/touch --full` where no interaction needed
- **Revisit if**: Volume of domains makes serial sweeps impractical

## 2026-03-09: Skills over scripts (Claude Code project-local skills)
- **Context**: Implementation approach for orchestrator commands
- **Alternatives considered**: Shell scripts, Python scripts, standalone CLI tool
- **Deciding factors**: Skills run in the same Claude Code session, no separate process management; skills can use all Claude Code tools; easier iteration
- **Revisit if**: Skills become too complex for inline implementation; need persistent background processes

## 2026-03-09: Staged writes — agents never write to canonical files directly
- **Context**: Memory and decision integrity across sessions
- **Alternatives considered**: Direct agent writes to MEMORY.md/DECISIONS.md
- **Deciding factors**: Human review gate ensures accuracy; distiller pipeline allows conflict detection; accidental overwrite risk too high
- **Revisit if**: Auto-approve mode becomes trusted enough for low-stakes domains

## 2026-03-09: Window-per-domain tmux model
- **Context**: UI layout for firehose sessions
- **Alternatives considered**: Pane reuse, single-window with content swap
- **Deciding factors**: tmux tab bar becomes sweep history; no fragile pane reuse; completed domains visible as tabs; natural navigation
- **Revisit if**: Tab bar becomes cluttered with many domains
- **Status**: SUPERSEDED by 2026-03-10 decision below

## 2026-03-10: VS Code workspace-per-domain viewport (replaces tmux/neovim)
- **Context**: Revisiting the viewport layer. Neovim/LazyVim + tmux required a Lua orchestrator module, tmux automation scripts, and shell wrappers — significant custom glue. Explored Cowork as an alternative; it lacks programmatic session creation and multi-file viewport. Discovered Claude Code's CLI flags (`--add-dir`, `--append-system-prompt-file`, `--session-id`) and VS Code workspace features (`folderOpen` tasks, `code` CLI for tab management) provide the viewport natively.
- **Alternatives considered**: Neovim + tmux (original), Cowork sessions, Cursor IDE
- **Deciding factors**: VS Code workspace files auto-open context files as tabs, launch Claude Code via `folderOpen` task, and support markdown preview — no custom code needed. The `code --new-window domain.code-workspace` command is the single entry point. Eliminates Phase 1 and Phase 2 glue code entirely. Agent can surface files mid-session via `code <file>` through Bash.
- **Revisit if**: VS Code `code` CLI becomes unreliable from within the integrated terminal; need for cross-domain visibility within a single viewport; VS Code performance degrades with many domain windows open

## 2026-03-10: Claude Code hooks for automatic context loading
- **Context**: Need the read-on-entry flow (PROFILE → MEMORY → DECISIONS → STATE) to happen automatically, not via prompt instruction
- **Alternatives considered**: Manual prompt ("read my context files"), CLAUDE.md instruction, skill-based loading
- **Deciding factors**: SessionStart hooks are deterministic — guaranteed to execute, not LLM-dependent. Hook stdout is injected directly as context. Fast, reliable, no wasted tokens on tool calls.
- **Revisit if**: Hook output size limits become a problem for large MEMORY.md files; SessionStart hook reliability issues (known bug with new conversations as of early 2026)

## 2026-03-10: Headless `claude -p` for distillation (isolated context)
- **Context**: Distiller must run without the working session's conversation history to avoid completion bias
- **Alternatives considered**: Cowork subagent, in-session distillation, separate API call
- **Deciding factors**: `claude -p` with `--system-prompt-file` gives a clean invocation with no shared context. Same tooling as interactive sessions (skills, filesystem access). The disk boundary between working agent and distiller is a debiasing mechanism, not just an implementation convenience.
- **Revisit if**: Claude Code provides a native "isolated subagent" primitive that's cleaner than `claude -p`

## 2026-03-10: Agent teams NOT used for cross-domain orchestration
- **Context**: Evaluated Claude Code agent teams (experimental) for firehose sweep coordination
- **Alternatives considered**: Agent team lead coordinates domain teammates
- **Deciding factors**: Agent teams share a working directory and task list — designed for multiple agents on ONE problem, not isolation between domains. Messaging system would allow domain context leakage. Agent teams are appropriate within a single domain (e.g., parallel research) but not as the firehose sweep mechanism.
- **Revisit if**: Agent teams gain per-teammate working directory isolation

## 2026-03-10: touch-domain as universal modal command
- **Context**: Touch was a health check. New requirement: touch should also be the entry point for domain creation. When you touch a path that doesn't exist, it should bootstrap a new domain. The bootstrapping conversation itself becomes the first session artifact.
- **Alternatives considered**: Separate `create-domain` command, manual scaffolding then touch
- **Deciding factors**: One command to rule them all. `touch-domain` inspects state and picks the right action: light validation, full profile regen, or new domain onboarding. Explicit flags (`--full`, `--new`) for when you want to force a mode. Default is smart — prompts for escalation when conditions warrant. Designed for extensibility: more `--options` will emerge.
- **Revisit if**: The modal behavior becomes confusing or unpredictable; flags proliferate beyond usability

## 2026-03-10: Git-aware domains with standard remote conventions
- **Context**: Domains should be version-controlled by default. Git provides an audit trail for agentic changes, ensures work is not lost, and enables the bare-repo-on-server convention Richard uses across projects.
- **Alternatives considered**: No git integration (leave it to the user), per-domain opt-in only
- **Deciding factors**: Git is assumed for all domains unless explicitly exempted. Global defaults define the standard setup (bare repo on configured primary server, optional secondary mirror). Per-domain overrides in agent.md. Touch checks git status as part of housekeeping — surfaces concerns, doesn't silently fix. `touch-domain --new` initializes git as part of bootstrapping. Environment-specific settings (server hostnames, paths) live in `firehose.local.md`, not in the portable spec.
- **Revisit if**: Domains emerge that genuinely shouldn't be git repos (ephemeral scratch domains?); bare repo convention becomes impractical for some repo types

+- **Context**: Clarifying the command structure as the system scope expanded beyond the original "firehose" sweep concept. Need clean separation between kit management, viewport launching, session capture, and memory processing.
+- **Alternatives considered**: Everything under `/firehose` umbrella, separate commands per function with inconsistent naming

+- **Deciding factors**: Each command maps to a distinct concern and operational posture. `touch-domain` = objective kit management. `open-kit` = subjective viewport launch (with `--cursor`, `--terminal` as viewport targets). `checkpoint` = in-session capture. `distill` = isolated post-session processing. Clean separation means each can evolve independently. `touch-domain` and `distill` are objective (operate from outside). `checkpoint` is subjective (operates from inside). `open-kit` is the transition between them.
+- **Revisit if**: Commands need to compose in ways the separation prevents; a fifth concern emerges that doesn't fit

+- **Context**: "Firehose" was originally the name for the whole system and the serial sweep command. As the command taxonomy crystallized, firehose became one feature among several — and a different kind of feature: not kit management or viewport launching, but strategic scanning across all domains to surface what needs attention.
+- **Alternatives considered**: Keep firehose as the system name, implement sweep immediately

+- **Deciding factors**: The core commands (touch-domain, open-kit, checkpoint, distill) need to be proven in daily use before building the sweep. Firehose as attention-direction is a System 4 function (VSM) — intelligence, looking outward and forward. Different from System 2/3 (coordination/control, which is touch-domain) and System 1 (operations, which is open-kit + checkpoint). Design deferred until core is stable.
+- **Revisit if**: The need for cross-domain scanning becomes urgent before core commands are proven
+## 2026-03-10: Domain kit as foundational concept (variety-agent-design lineage)

+- **Context**: The domain kit concept originates from cybernetics-grounded analysis of human-agent systems (Ashby's Law of Requisite Variety, Beer's Viable System Model). The term "domain kit" was coined in this project to describe the complete set of domain-specific resources a model is given access to — a viable system, not just a prompt.
+- **Alternatives considered**: Using mainstream terminology ("context engineering", "agent skills", "prompt templates")

+- **Deciding factors**: Existing terms don't capture the governance dimension — the session-distillation loop, variety engineering, completeness bias management, the domain kit as an ontological space. "Domain kit" does real work that existing vocabulary doesn't. The .context/ directory structure is a direct implementation of the domain kit concept: Configuration (agent.md, README.md) + State (MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md, STATE.md, sessions/).
+- **Revisit if**: "Domain kit" collides with an established term in the field; a better term emerges

# State — firehose

## Current Status: Command taxonomy crystallized, ready for implementation

The system has a clear command taxonomy: `touch-domain` (kit management), `open-kit` (viewport launch), `checkpoint` (session capture), `distill` (memory processing). Firehose is reframed as a future attention-direction feature. The variety-agent-design theoretical foundation is integrated. Specs are revised and aligned.

## What's Done

- **Command taxonomy**: four commands, four concerns, cleanly separated. Documented in orchestrator-architecture.md and README.md.
- **`touch-domain`** spec complete: default/--full/--new modes, --no-touchy and -y modifiers, git state precheck (6 states), extensible design
- **`open-kit`** spec complete: `--cursor` and `--terminal` viewport targets, workspace file launch
- **`checkpoint`** and **`distill`** specs carried forward from earlier work, aligned with new taxonomy
- **Variety-agent-design lineage**: domain kit as viable system (Ashby, Beer, VSM) integrated into README and DECISIONS
- Viewport pivot (2026-03-10): VS Code workspace-per-domain via `open-kit --cursor`
- Git conventions: agnostic spec with environment-specific settings in `firehose.local.md`
- All three spec documents revised and consistent
- README.md written with framing, user story, and command reference

## What's In Progress

- **Workspace file template** — `domain.code-workspace` format defined in spec, needs to be generated by `touch-domain --full`
- **SessionStart hook** — design for automatic context loading on domain entry
- **Distiller prompt** — `distiller-prompt.md` for headless `claude -p` invocation
- **Global config** — `~/.firehose/config.md` (or similar) for git conventions, remote defaults, system-wide settings

## What's Blocked / Open

- Repo itself is not yet a git repo
- No live registry — identified 4 touched domains + ~4 candidates
- Skills need update to match new command taxonomy (touch-domain modes, open-kit implementation)
- SessionStart hook implementation and testing
- Session transcript capture — Claude Code native persistence vs `claudeProcessWrapper`
- Verify `code` CLI works reliably from within VS Code integrated terminal
- Global config format needs design
- `variety-agent-design.md` lives in cursus, not in this repo — decide whether to copy or reference

## Next Steps

1. Initialize repo with git, set up bare on primary server
2. Copy or link `variety-agent-design.md` into this repo
3. Design global config format
4. Implement `touch-domain` skill with all modes
5. Implement `open-kit` as a skill or shell script
6. Write `SessionStart` hook
7. Write registry
8. Test end-to-end: `touch-domain --new /path/to/test-domain` → onboarding → git → workspace → `open-kit test-domain --cursor`

## Last Updated: 2026-03-10

input.content:
# Domain Kit Engineering

This repo defines the domain kit architecture for managing durable human-agent collaboration across multiple domains. See `variety-agent-design.md` for the cybernetics-grounded theoretical foundation.

Key design documents:

- `orchestrator-architecture.md` – commands (`touch-domain`, `open-kit`, `checkpoint`, `distill`), viewport, runtime, hooks, git conventions.
- `domain-convention.md` – what a domain is, how `.context/` is structured, git convention, lifecycle.
- `distiller-spec.md` – how session artifacts become canonical `MEMORY.md` and `DECISIONS.md`.

## Command taxonomy

- **`touch-domain`** – kit management. Structural health, git state, profiling, scaffolding, bootstrapping. Objective.
- **`open-kit`** – viewport launch. Opens a domain for interactive work in a specified viewport (`--cursor`, `--terminal`).
- **`checkpoint`** – session capture. Structured snapshots during interactive work.
- **`distill`** – memory processing. Isolated post-session distillation. Objective, debiased.

## When working inside a domain kit

- Treat a **domain** as a folder with:
  - A root `README.md` describing what the domain is.
  - A `.context/` directory containing `PROFILE.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, `STATE.md`, `agent.md`, and `sessions/`.
- Follow the read/write flow from the specs:
  - **Read on entry:** `PROFILE.md → MEMORY.md → DECISIONS.md → STATE.md`.
  - **Write on exit:** use checkpoints and the distiller instead of editing memory/decisions directly.

## Claude Code skills for this repo

This project includes project-local skills under `.claude/skills/` that implement domain kit behavior:

- `/domain-convention` – reference for the domain layout and file roles.
- `/touch-domain` – structural validation and scaffolding for a domain's `.context/` directory.
- `/touch-full-domain` – full touch with PROFILE.md and workspace file generation.
- `/checkpoint` – writes structured session checkpoints and optional memory drafts into `.context/sessions/`.
- `/distill-domain` – runs the distillation pipeline for a single domain, producing `.proposed` updates.
- `/firehose` – (future) coordinates attention-direction sweep across domains.

## Workflow

1. `touch-domain <path>` — validate or bootstrap a domain.
2. `open-kit <domain> --cursor` — open the domain viewport for interactive work.
3. During work, use `/checkpoint` and `/checkpoint --close` to capture session artifacts.
4. After sessions, run `distill <domain>` to propose updates to `MEMORY.md` and `DECISIONS.md`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Firehose / Domain Orchestrator Conventions

This repo defines the **firehose** domain/orchestrator architecture used to manage long-lived work across multiple domains.

Key design documents:

- `orchestrator-architecture.md` – commands like `/touch`, `/firehose`, `/checkpoint`, sweep state, and VS Code workspace viewport.
- `domain-convention.md` – what a domain is and how `.context/` is structured.
- `distiller-spec.md` – how session artifacts become canonical `MEMORY.md` and `DECISIONS.md`.

## When working inside a firehose-managed domain

- Treat a **domain** as a folder with:
  - A root `README.md` describing what the domain is.
  - A `.context/` directory containing `PROFILE.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, `STATE.md`, `agent.md`, and `sessions/`.
- Follow the read/write flow from the specs:
  - **Read on entry:** `PROFILE.md → MEMORY.md → DECISIONS.md → STATE.md`.
  - **Write on exit:** use checkpoints and the distiller instead of editing memory/decisions directly.

## Claude Code skills for this repo

This project includes project-local skills under `.claude/skills/` that implement the firehose behavior:

- `/domain-convention` – reference for the domain layout and file roles.
- `/touch-domain` – structural validation and scaffolding for a domain’s `.context/` directory (no synthesis).
- `/touch-full-domain` – full touch that regenerates `PROFILE.md` from canonical files plus a light scan.
- `/checkpoint` – writes structured session checkpoints and optional memory drafts into `.context/sessions/`.
- `/distill-domain` – runs the distillation pipeline for a single domain, producing `.proposed` updates.
- `/firehose` – coordinates a serial sweep across domains using a markdown registry.

When you want to use the firehose workflow in a real codebase:

1. Create or identify a domain folder and run `/touch-domain` there.
2. Use `/touch-full-domain` to generate `PROFILE.md`.
3. During work, use `/checkpoint` and `/checkpoint --close` to capture session artifacts.
4. After sessions, run `/distill-domain` to propose updates to `MEMORY.md` and `DECISIONS.md`.
5. Use `/firehose` (and a `firehose/REGISTRY.md` file) to coordinate serial sweeps across many domains.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Analysis:
Let me chronologically trace through this entire conversation to capture all the key details.

1. **Opening**: User asked me to look at their repo, particularly the skills like "touch". I explored the firehose repo at `/home/richard/sources/firehose/` and provided a comprehensive summary of the domain orchestrator architecture.

2. **Cowork as viewport**: User proposed using Cowork instead of neovim/lazyvim as the viewport. I suggested it could work, mapping skills directly. User corrected me on the distiller - it MUST be a separate subagent for objectivity/debiasing (completion bias of active agent).

3. **Cowork limitations explored**: User asked about spawning new sessions, separate context windows per domain. I was honest that Cowork can spawn background subagents but can't create interactive sessions programmatically.

4. **Web search phase**: User asked me to search for hooks/capabilities. I discovered:
   - Claude Code hooks (SessionStart, Stop, PostToolUse, etc.)
   - Claude Code CLI flags (--add-dir, --append-system-prompt-file, --session-id, etc.)
   - Agent Teams (experimental, with TeammateTool)
   - VS Code extension capabilities

5. **Agent teams context leakage**: User correctly identified that agent teams share context and are for single-domain work, not cross-domain orchestration.

6. **VS Code as viewport**: I proposed VS Code with Claude Code in integrated terminal. User said they were told Claude couldn't open tabs. I searched and found the `code` CLI workaround via Bash tool.

7. **Workspace files**: Key breakthrough - VS Code workspace files with `folderOpen` tasks can auto-open files and launch Claude Code. `code --new-window domain.code-workspace` is the entry point.

8. **First spec update round**: Updated orchestrator-architecture.md (viewport, runtime, subagent, implementation), domain-convention.md (session logs), distiller-spec.md (isolation, invocation), DECISIONS.md (5 new decisions), STATE.md (full rewrite), CLAUDE.md.

9. **Consistency check**: Ran a subagent to verify cross-file consistency. Found and fixed 5 stale references (CLAUDE.md tmux reference, claudeProcessWrapper framing, wrapper-captured terminology).

10. **User story discussion**: User wanted to understand entry point (Claude Code terminal for launchpad, VS Code extension for domain work). Discussion of extension vs terminal delta.

11. **touch-domain evolution**: User proposed touch as modal command with --full, --new, --no-touchy. Touch becomes universal domain entry point including creation.

12. **Git awareness**: User wants git state precheck with 6 states (diverged, behind, ahead, clean, no-remote, not-a-repo). Environment-agnostic specs with `firehose.local.md` for specifics.

13. **User corrections on git states**:
   - Diverged: open in IDE to resolve (future)
   - Behind: prompt "canonical version is on server, work on that or pull here?"
   - Always surface state to user
   - Prompt before writing to server, -y to skip
   - Don't encode environment specifics (fluffy) into portable spec

14. **PROFILE vs STATE discussion**: Resolved as PROFILE = state + context. STATE stays as cheap writable input, PROFILE as expensive synthesized output.

15. **variety-agent-design.md**: User uploaded cybernetics document. Domain Kit defined as "basic unit of governance" with Configuration (model, prompt, persona, context) + State. Maps directly to .context/ structure. Distiller = Objective Reasoning.

16. **Command taxonomy crystallized**:
   - `touch-domain` = kit management (objective)
   - `open-kit --cursor` = viewport launch (transition to subjective)
   - `checkpoint` = session capture (subjective)
   - `distill` = memory processing (objective, isolated)
   - `firehose` = future attention-direction feature (System 4)

17. **Final spec update round**: Rewrote orchestrator-architecture.md with new title "Domain Kit Architecture", added open-kit command, reframed firehose. Updated README.md, DECISIONS.md (4 new entries), STATE.md, CLAUDE.md.

18. **User's final question**: Asked whether I used the cybernetics stuff in the materials or just as inspiration. Then the summary request came.

Key user corrections/feedback throughout:
- Distiller MUST be isolated (debiasing, not convenience)
- Agent teams share context = bad for cross-domain
- Don't encode environment specifics into portable spec
- Touch default may need model calls (not strictly free)
- Default touch is NOT read-only (creates scaffolding)
- Need --no-touchy for pure diagnostic
- Git state assessment must come BEFORE any touch logic
- Prompt before writing to server
- PROFILE = state + context
- Firehose is a different thing from the system name
- Four commands, four concerns taxonomy

Files modified:
- orchestrator-architecture.md (multiple rounds)
- domain-convention.md (multiple rounds)
- distiller-spec.md (multiple rounds)
- .context/DECISIONS.md (multiple rounds)
- .context/STATE.md (multiple rounds)
- CLAUDE.md (multiple rounds)
- README.md (created, then rewritten)
- firehose.local.md (created)

The user's last substantive message before the summary request was asking whether I used the cybernetics content directly in the materials. I had not yet answered this question when the summary was requested.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user (Richard) asked me to review his firehose domain orchestrator repo and help evolve it. The conversation progressed through several major phases:
   - **Evaluate Cowork as a viewport** alternative to neovim/lazyvim for the firehose system
   - **Discover Claude Code capabilities** (hooks, CLI flags, agent teams, VS Code extension) via web search
   - **Identify VS Code workspace files** as the viewport solution (auto-open tabs, launch Claude Code via `folderOpen` tasks)
   - **Revise all specs** to reflect the new VS Code + Claude Code runtime (replacing neovim/tmux)
   - **Elevate `touch-domain`** from health check to universal modal command (default/--full/--new/--no-touchy)
   - **Add git awareness** with 6-state precheck, environment-agnostic spec, and `firehose.local.md` for local settings
   - **Crystallize command taxonomy**: `touch-domain` (kit management), `open-kit` (viewport launch), `checkpoint` (session capture), `distill` (memory processing)
   - **Integrate variety-agent-design.md** cybernetics foundation (Ashby, Beer, VSM) and "domain kit" as foundational concept
   - **Reframe firehose** from system name to future attention-direction feature (System 4 in VSM terms)
   - **Keep specs environment-agnostic** — all Richard-specific details (fluffy.geekazoid.net, paths) in `firehose.local.md`

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Domain Kit**: coined term for the complete set of domain-specific resources a model gets access to (prompts, personas, context, state, memories, tools, skills). Based on cybernetics (Ashby's Law of Requisite Variety, Beer's VSM).
   - **`.context/` directory convention**: PROFILE.md, MEMORY.md, DECISIONS.md, STATE.md, agent.md, sessions/
   - **VS Code workspace files** (`domain.code-workspace`): `folderOpen` tasks auto-open context files and launch Claude Code
   - **Claude Code hooks**: SessionStart (context injection), Stop (session summaries), PostToolUse (constraints)
   - **Claude Code CLI flags**: `--append-system-prompt-file`, `--session-id`, `--add-dir`, `-p` (print/headless mode), `--system-prompt-file`
   - **`claudeProcessWrapper`** VS Code setting for session logging
   - **Distiller isolation**: `claude -p` in separate invocation — debiasing mechanism against completion bias, NOT just implementation convenience
   - **Agent Teams**: experimental Claude Code feature — shares context/working directory, suitable for WITHIN a domain only, NOT for cross-domain orchestration
   - **Git state precheck**: 6 states (Diverged, Behind, Ahead, Clean, No remote, Not a repo) assessed BEFORE any touch logic
   - **VSM mapping**: Domain Kits = System 1, coordination = System 2, touch-domain = System 3, firehose = System 4, human = System 5
   - **`code` CLI from Bash**: Claude Code can open files as VS Code tabs via `code <file>` through the Bash tool
   - **PROFILE.md = state + context**: synthesizes STATE + MEMORY + DECISIONS + README + repo scan. STATE stays as cheap writable input.

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/orchestrator-architecture.md`**
     - Central spec document, revised multiple times. Now titled "Domain Kit Architecture"
     - Contains all four command specs (touch-domain, open-kit, checkpoint, distill/firehose-future)
     - Git conventions with 6-state precheck table
     - VS Code viewport spec with workspace file template
     - Hooks section (SessionStart, Stop, PostToolUse)
     - Implementation phases renamed: Phase 1 = Core Commands, Phase 2 = Memory Lifecycle, Phase 3 = Attention Direction
     - Key workspace file template:
     ```json
     {
       "folders": [
         { "path": "." },
         { "path": ".context", "name": "Domain Context" }
       ],
       "tasks": {
         "version": "2.0.0",
         "tasks": [
           {
             "label": "Open Domain Context",
             "type": "shell",
             "command": "code .context/PROFILE.md .context/STATE.md .context/MEMORY.md .context/DECISIONS.md",
             "presentation": { "reveal": "silent" },
             "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }
           },
           {
             "label": "Start Domain Session",
             "type": "shell",
             "command": "claude --append-system-prompt-file .context/agent.md 'Run /touch-full-domain and brief me'",
             "presentation": { "reveal": "always", "panel": "new" },
             "runOptions": { "runOn": "folderOpen" }
           }
         ]
       },
       "settings": {}
     }
     ```
     - Key command usage block:
     ```
     touch-domain <path>              # smart touch
     touch-domain --full <path>       # full profile regeneration
     touch-domain --new <path>        # new domain bootstrapping
     touch-domain --no-touchy <path>  # read-only diagnostic
     touch-domain -y <path>           # suppress prompts
     open-kit cashflow --cursor       # open in Cursor/VS Code
     open-kit cashflow --terminal     # open in terminal Claude Code
     ```

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/domain-convention.md`**
     - Domain structure spec. Added Git Convention section, rewrote Lifecycle section
     - Session log capture updated from "wrapper script" to Claude Code native persistence
     - Domain Creation now flows through `touch-domain --new`
     - Git convention specifies what belongs in git (.context/ canonical files, sessions) and what doesn't (PROFILE.md, .proposed files)

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/distiller-spec.md`**
     - Added Isolation Requirement section documenting debiasing rationale
     - Key invocation pattern:
     ```bash
     claude -p --system-prompt-file distiller-prompt.md \
            "Distill domain at /path/to/domain"
     ```
     - Updated trigger modes to reference Claude Code hooks
     - Updated implementation phases for VS Code diff/merge tooling

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/.context/DECISIONS.md`**
     - Grew from 5 entries to 13 entries across the session
     - Key new entries: VS Code workspace-per-domain viewport, Claude Code hooks, headless `claude -p` for distillation, agent teams NOT for cross-domain, touch-domain as universal modal command, git-aware domains, command taxonomy (4 concerns/4 commands), firehose reframed as attention-direction, domain kit as foundational concept

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/.context/STATE.md`**
     - Rewritten multiple times to reflect evolving understanding
     - Current status: "Command taxonomy crystallized, ready for implementation"
     - Lists next steps starting with git init and ending with end-to-end test

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/CLAUDE.md`**
     - Rewritten to reflect "Domain Kit Engineering" framing
     - Command taxonomy front and center
     - Updated skill references and workflow section

   - **`/home/richard/sources/firehose/README.md`** (created)
     - Captures the "why" framing: infrastructure for durable human-agent collaboration
     - Full user story walking through scan → open-kit → work → checkpoint → distill → new domain flows

     - Cybernetics-grounded theoretical foundation for domain kit concept

     - Domain Kit defined as Configuration (model, prompt, persona, context) + State (session history, reasoning, telemetry)
     - Objective Reasoning concept = the distiller architecture
     - Completeness bias analysis explaining why thorough domain kits matter

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **Distiller isolation**: I initially suggested the distiller could run in the same Cowork session. User corrected: "there is a reason why distiller is a separate subagent. the idea here is objectivity. the completion bias of the active agent is going to influence the reasoning explanation." Fixed by maintaining strict isolation boundary in all subsequent design.
   - **Agent teams for cross-domain**: I proposed agent teams for firehose sweep. User correctly identified context leakage risk: "agent teams share context... my understanding was that agent teams are ideal for single domain work." Fixed by recording this as a decision and never using agent teams for cross-domain.
   - **Environment-specific references in specs**: I embedded "fluffy.geekazoid.net" throughout the specs. User said: "lets make sure that we don't encode things specific to my environment into the skill spec/definition." Fixed by creating `firehose.local.md` and scrubbing all specs of environment-specific references.
   - **Default touch as read-only**: I stated default touch has no model calls and is read-only. User pushed back: "I'm not convinced that default has no model calls" and "are we saying that light touch is read-only?" — "No, I don't think we are." Fixed by clarifying default touch may use model calls for reasoning about what it found, and does write (scaffolding repairs, workspace file generation).
   - **Consistency check findings**: Subagent found 5 stale references after first spec update round (CLAUDE.md "tmux layout", claudeProcessWrapper framing, "wrapper-captured" terminology, mixed old/new terminology). All fixed.
   - **File read error**: Tried to edit CLAUDE.md without reading it first, got "File has not been read yet" error. Fixed by reading the file first.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Viewport problem**: User unhappy with neovim, explored Cowork, discovered it can't programmatically create sessions. Web search revealed Claude Code CLI flags + VS Code workspace files as the solution. `code --new-window domain.code-workspace` with `folderOpen` tasks provides the entire viewport without custom code.
   - **Session logging without wrapper**: Originally needed a custom wrapper script. Discovered `claudeProcessWrapper` VS Code setting and Claude Code native session persistence both work with the extension, not just terminal.
   - **Opening files as tabs**: User was told Claude can't open tabs. Found the `code` CLI workaround via Bash tool — Claude Code can run `code <file>` to open tabs in the same VS Code window.
   - **Command naming**: Went through firefighting metaphors (deploy, respond, train, spike) — none fit. Landed on `open-kit` from the domain kit concept, with `--cursor`/`--terminal` viewport targets.
   - **PROFILE vs STATE redundancy**: Resolved as complementary — STATE is the cheap, frequently-written input; PROFILE is the expensive, periodically-regenerated synthesis of STATE + everything else.

My design called for the ability to session log, which we were going to do with a wrapper... But I wonder if this cowork tool is really without any hooks we can use."
   - "okay so you have figured out a lot of the subagent stuff. am i right that agent teams share context?... I'm not sold on tmux split pane... still wondering if we can use claude code + local tools for this. or modify cowork to our aims"

content.thinking: The user's last question was: "Did you use any of the cybernetics stuff in the materials or just inspiration?" I need to answer this.

content.text:
To answer your question — it's both, but leaning more toward inspiration and framing than direct incorporation.

Here's where the cybernetics content shows up directly: the README.md references variety-agent-design.md as the theoretical foundation and describes the domain kit as "a complete ontological space within which an agent reasons." DECISIONS.md has an explicit entry documenting the lineage — "Domain kit as foundational concept (variety-agent-design lineage)" — and uses the VSM system mapping in the firehose entry (firehose = System 4, touch-domain = System 2/3). The orchestrator-architecture.md overview references the theoretical foundation too.

But the specs themselves don't use the cybernetics vocabulary heavily. You won't find "variety," "attenuation," "amplification," or "requisite variety" as working terms in the command specs or the domain convention. The specs speak in plain engineering language — commands, modes, git states, file conventions. The cybernetics provides the *why* (why isolation matters for the distiller, why domain kits need to be complete, why the firehose sweep is a different kind of function than touch-domain), but the *how* is expressed without jargon.

So the theoretical document is doing its job as a foundation document — it grounds the design decisions without requiring readers of the specs to understand Beer or Ashby. That said, the document itself still lives at its original location on fluffy in the cursus inbox rather than in this repo, which is step 2 on the STATE.md next steps list.

Worth deciding: do you want more of the cybernetics language surfaced in the specs themselves, or is the current approach right — foundation document for the theory, plain language for the implementation?

# State — firehose

## Current Status: Command taxonomy crystallized, ready for implementation

The system has a clear command taxonomy: `touch-domain` (kit management), `open-kit` (viewport launch), `checkpoint` (session capture), `distill` (memory processing). Firehose is reframed as a future attention-direction feature. The variety-agent-design theoretical foundation is integrated. Specs are revised and aligned.

## What's Done

- **Command taxonomy**: four commands, four concerns, cleanly separated. Documented in orchestrator-architecture.md and README.md.
- **`touch-domain`** spec complete: default/--full/--new modes, --no-touchy and -y modifiers, git state precheck (6 states), extensible design
- **`open-kit`** spec complete: `--cursor` and `--terminal` viewport targets, workspace file launch
- **`checkpoint`** and **`distill`** specs carried forward from earlier work, aligned with new taxonomy
- **Variety-agent-design lineage**: domain kit as viable system (Ashby, Beer, VSM) integrated into README and DECISIONS
- Viewport pivot (2026-03-10): VS Code workspace-per-domain via `open-kit --cursor`
- Git conventions: agnostic spec with environment-specific settings in `firehose.local.md`
- All three spec documents revised and consistent
- README.md written with framing, user story, and command reference

## What's In Progress

- **Workspace file template** — `domain.code-workspace` format defined in spec, needs to be generated by `touch-domain --full`
- **SessionStart hook** — design for automatic context loading on domain entry
- **Distiller prompt** — `distiller-prompt.md` for headless `claude -p` invocation
- **Global config** — `~/.firehose/config.md` (or similar) for git conventions, remote defaults, system-wide settings

## What's Blocked / Open

- Repo itself is not yet a git repo
- No live registry — identified 4 touched domains + ~4 candidates
- Skills need update to match new command taxonomy (touch-domain modes, open-kit implementation)
- SessionStart hook implementation and testing
- Session transcript capture — Claude Code native persistence vs `claudeProcessWrapper`
- Verify `code` CLI works reliably from within VS Code integrated terminal
- Global config format needs design
- `variety-agent-design.md` lives in cursus, not in this repo — decide whether to copy or reference

## Next Steps

1. Initialize repo with git, set up bare on primary server
2. Copy or link `variety-agent-design.md` into this repo
3. Design global config format
4. Implement `touch-domain` skill with all modes
5. Implement `open-kit` as a skill or shell script
6. Write `SessionStart` hook
7. Write registry
8. Test end-to-end: `touch-domain --new /path/to/test-domain` → onboarding → git → workspace → `open-kit test-domain --cursor`

## Last Updated: 2026-03-10

content.content:
---
name: touch-domain
description: Validate and scaffold a firehose domain's .context/ directory and required files without doing any content synthesis. Use to create or structurally check a domain.
disable-model-invocation: true
model: haiku
argument-hint: "[domain-path]"
---

You are implementing the **`/touch`** command from `orchestrator-architecture.md` as a Claude Code skill.

This skill is **structural only**:

- It ensures the domain's `.context/` directory and required files exist.
- It validates pointer integrity in `agent.md` if present.
- It may create minimal, spec-compliant scaffolding files.
- It **must not** perform any heavy content synthesis or regenerate `PROFILE.md`.

## How to interpret arguments

- If `$ARGUMENTS` is provided, treat `$0` as the **domain root path** (relative or absolute).
- If no arguments are provided, treat the **current working directory** as the domain root.

Always normalize to the domain root before operating.

## Steps

1. **Locate the domain root**
   - Resolve `$0` if present; otherwise use the current working directory.
   - Confirm that `README.md` exists at the domain root. If not present, create a minimal placeholder and note in comments that it should be human-authored.

2. **Ensure `.context/` layout**
   - Under the domain root, ensure a `.context/` directory exists.
   - Within `.context/`, ensure the following files and directories exist, creating minimal skeletons if needed:
     - `MEMORY.md`
     - `DECISIONS.md`
     - `STATE.md`
     - `PROFILE.md`
     - `agent.md`
     - `sessions/` directory (and `processed/` subdirectory inside it)
   - Skeleton contents should be as small as possible and clearly marked as placeholders that follow the structure described in `domain-convention.md`.

3. **Validate `agent.md` pointers**
   - If `agent.md` exists, read it and check any referenced context files (such as `MEMORY.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, `STATE.md`, or other listed paths) actually exist.
   - If a referenced file is missing, create an empty, clearly labeled placeholder consistent with the convention.

4. **Report structural health**
   - Summarize the results in chat and, optionally, in a small report file at:
     - `.context/TOUCH-REPORT.md`
   - The report should include:
     - Domain root path
     - Whether each required file existed or was created
     - Any pointer issues found and fixed in `agent.md`

5. **Do not regenerate PROFILE.md**
   - This skill **must not** rewrite `PROFILE.md` beyond creating a minimal placeholder if it was missing.
   - Regeneration of `PROFILE.md` belongs to the full-touch skill (`/touch-full-domain`), which performs content synthesis.

When unsure about the expected structure or file roles, read `domain-convention.md` in the repo root and follow its definitions.

content.content:
---
name: touch-full-domain
description: Run a full touch on a firehose domain by regenerating PROFILE.md from canonical files and a light repo scan. Use when you want an up-to-date briefing for a domain.
disable-model-invocation: true
argument-hint: "[domain-path]"
---

You are implementing the **`/touch --full`** command from `orchestrator-architecture.md` as a Claude Code skill.

This skill is **content-generating** but still bounded:

- It performs all structural checks that `/touch` does.
- It reads the canonical domain files and performs a light scan of domain contents.
- It **regenerates** `.context/PROFILE.md` as a derived briefing document.

## How to interpret arguments

- If `$ARGUMENTS` is provided, treat `$0` as the **domain root path** (relative or absolute).
- If no arguments are provided, treat the **current working directory** as the domain root.

Always normalize to the domain root before operating.

## Steps

1. **Run structural touch first**
   - Conceptually reuse the behavior of the `/touch-domain` skill:
     - Ensure `.context/` and required files exist.
     - Validate `agent.md` pointers and create placeholders if needed.
   - You do not need to invoke the other skill directly; you can reapply the same logic here.

2. **Load canonical context**
   - Read the following files from the domain:
     - Root `README.md`
     - `.context/STATE.md`
     - `.context/MEMORY.md`
     - `.context/DECISIONS.md`
     - `.context/agent.md`
   - If any are missing or only contain placeholder content, still proceed but note that in the generated profile.

3. **Perform a light domain scan**
   - Using read/search tools, perform a **targeted** scan of the domain contents:
     - Focus on key implementation files referenced in `README.md`, `STATE.md`, `MEMORY.md`, or `agent.md`.
     - Avoid exhaustively reading very large trees; sample or skim, and rely on the canonical files for most signal.

4. **Regenerate `.context/PROFILE.md`**
   - Overwrite `.context/PROFILE.md` with a fresh, single-file **briefing document** that:
     - Summarizes what the domain is and does.
     - Calls out key components, entry points, and important files.
     - Highlights important decisions or constraints from `DECISIONS.md` and `MEMORY.md`.
     - Notes current status and open threads from `STATE.md`.
     - Mentions any gaps or obvious missing pieces in the canonical files.
   - Make it explicit in the profile that it is **derived** and should not be hand-edited:
     - Include a short header like: “This file is generated by `/touch-full-domain`. Edit README/STATE/MEMORY/DECISIONS instead.”

5. **Summarize in chat**
   - After regenerating `PROFILE.md`, print a short summary for the user:
     - Domain root
     - Timestamp of the full touch
     - Any notable gaps or warnings you detected (e.g., missing DECISIONS.md entries, very stale STATE.md).

content.text:
OK, here's the honest assessment. You have specs, you have skills, but there's a gap between them. Let me lay out what's real, what's assumed, and what needs to happen.

**What's actually working right now:**
- Claude Code is installed and current (2.1.72)
- The skills exist and are structurally sound — `/touch-domain` and `/touch-full-domain` would work today if you ran them from Claude Code inside this repo
- The spec documents are coherent and aligned
- The `.context/` convention is defined and this repo itself follows it

**Assumptions that need verification:**

1. **`code` CLI from Claude Code** — we said Claude can run `code somefile.md` to open tabs. I haven't actually tested this on your machine. Easy to verify right now if you want.

2. **VS Code workspace `folderOpen` tasks** — the spec relies on tasks with `"runOn": "folderOpen"` to auto-open context files and launch Claude Code. This is a real VS Code feature, but we haven't tested whether it reliably launches `claude` in the integrated terminal. Worth a quick smoke test with a throwaway workspace file.

3. **`claude --append-system-prompt-file`** — we're counting on this flag to inject `agent.md` on session start. It exists in the docs, but has it shipped in your version? That's testable.

4. **`claudeProcessWrapper`** — the VS Code setting for session logging. Needs to be confirmed as available in your Claude Code extension version.

5. **`claude -p` for distillation** — headless mode for the distiller. This one I'm confident works, it's well-documented.

**What's actually blocking you from using this today:**

The repo isn't a git repo yet. That's step zero — not because the tools require it, but because git state precheck is baked into the touch-domain spec as the *first* thing that runs. Without git, touch will report "not-a-repo" every time, which is fine but means you're skipping the whole git awareness layer.

The skills also need updating to match the new taxonomy. The current `touch-domain` skill doesn't have `--new`, `--no-touchy`, `-y`, or the git precheck. It's the old structural-only version. And `open-kit` doesn't exist as a skill yet.

**So the practical path is:**

First, verify the assumptions — I can test 1 and 3 right now from here. Then `git init` and get the repo under version control. Then update `touch-domain` to the new modal spec and write `open-kit`. At that point you could actually do `touch-domain --new /path/to/some-domain` and `open-kit that-domain --cursor` for real.

Want me to start with the assumption verification?

# Firehose Local Environment Config
#
# This file contains environment-specific settings for THIS installation
# of the firehose system. It is NOT part of the portable spec.
# Each firehose user would have their own version of this file.

## Git Remote Conventions

### Primary bare repo (origin)
- Host: root@fluffy.geekazoid.net
- Path pattern: /mnt/user/git/<repository>.git
- Example: root@fluffy.geekazoid.net:/mnt/user/git/firehose.git

### Secondary mirror (optional, per-domain opt-in)
- Provider: GitHub (private repos)
- Org/user: TBD

## Known Domains

### Touched (confirmed)
- firehose — this repo
- cashflow
- systems-architectures
- systems-geekazoid

### Candidates (not yet touched)
- systems-harrklen
- cursus.local
- claude-collect
- francoeur/agent-portfolio

## Desktop Environment
- OS: Linux Mint, MATE, X11
- Editor: VS Code / Cursor
- Agent runtime: Claude Code CLI + VS Code extension

- fluffy.geekazoid.net is the primary server/NAS
- All bare repos live under /mnt/user/git/ on fluffy
